summary: skill_adoption_rate and fallback_trigger_rate use the all-event-types denominator, so adding governance/durability events mechanically deflates these rates even when skill/fallback activity is unchanged.
failure_scenario: compute_reliability_summary computes `skill_adoption_rate = skill_activate_count / COUNT(*)` over every event type for the agent (query.rs:591-597, 643). Because W19-P1 added 11 governance/durability event types that emit at high frequency (every retry attempt, every circuit transition, every policy eval), an agent that activates the same skills as before but now also emits hundreds of durability_retry_attempt rows will show a collapsed skill_adoption_rate on the dashboard — the metric measures 'fraction of audit log volume' rather than adoption, misleading operators into thinking skill usage dropped.

summary: A reminder whose batch disk status-update fails stays Pending on disk AND in fired_ids, so it is permanently skipped (never delivered, never retried) until gateway restart.
failure_scenario: In fire_due_reminders, all due reminders are inserted into fired_ids (line 614-618) and removed from the time wheel. After delivery, the batch status write goes through mutate_reminders (line 676). If that write fails (e.g. transient lockfile/disk error at line 698), the Err branch intentionally keeps the IDs in fired_ids to avoid re-delivery. But the on-disk status is still Pending. On the next reload (line 519) the reminder is Pending AND in fired_ids, so it is dropped from the wheel forever. The user's reminder is silently lost with no Failed status recorded, until the process restarts (which clears fired_ids). For a delivered-but-status-write-failed reminder this is the safe choice, but for a delivery that itself failed the reminder is neither retried nor surfaced.

summary: agent_callback reminder delivery holds a semaphore permit across an unbounded Claude LLM call plus a 2s retry sleep, so 4 slow callbacks stall every reminder delivery.
failure_scenario: MAX_CONCURRENT_DELIVER is 4. In deliver_reminder, AgentCallback mode calls call_claude_for_agent_with_type (line 777) while holding the permit acquired in fire_due_reminders (line 629). If 4 agent_callback reminders fire and their Claude invocations are slow/hung (rate-limited OAuth, PTY pool stall), all 4 permits are held; any additional due reminders — including cheap Direct-mode ones — block on sem.acquire() indefinitely. The retry path also sleeps 2s holding the permit (line 792). A handful of slow agent callbacks therefore delay or starve unrelated reminders for other agents/channels.

summary: handle_evolution_toggle joins agent_id into a path without is_valid_agent_id validation, allowing path traversal to read+overwrite arbitrary */agent.toml files.
failure_scenario: Unlike handle_agent_update/handle_agent_status which call is_valid_agent_id, evolution_toggle takes agent_id straight from arguments into home_dir.join("agents").join(agent_id) (line 3826) and later writes back to the same toml_path (line ~3976). An internal caller passing agent_id='../../../somewhere' that contains an agent.toml can read it, mutate an [evolution] field, and write it back — a traversal-scoped write primitive over any *.toml-bearing directory. Combined with the missing scope requirement (finding 1), no privilege is needed to reach it.

summary: handle_wiki_write performs no cross-agent visibility check, while handle_wiki_read does — any internal agent can write into another agent's wiki by passing agent_id.
failure_scenario: handle_wiki_read (line 7368) calls check_wiki_visibility before reading another agent's wiki. handle_wiki_write (line 7407) reads agent_id from arguments and resolves the target wiki dir with zero visibility/ownership check. An internal agent A can call wiki_write with agent_id="agent-B" and inject or overwrite pages (subject only to WIKI_RESERVED filename rules) inside agent B's wiki — a stored-content injection vector into another agent's RAG corpus, and an authorization asymmetry versus the read path.

summary: authenticate_from_env grants an all-scopes internal principal when no key env var and no [mcp_keys] are configured, a fail-open default for the stdio MCP path.
failure_scenario: On stdio transport, run_mcp_server calls authenticate_from_env. If DUDUCLAW_MCP_API_KEY is unset AND config.toml has no [mcp_keys] table (the out-of-box state), the function returns default_internal_principal() with MemoryRead/Write, WikiRead/Write, MessagingSend and Admin scopes and is_external=false. Only a tracing::warn! is emitted. Any local process able to speak to the MCP stdio server (e.g. a misconfigured Claude Code instance, or a compromised local tool) thus gets full Admin including audit_trail_query/reliability_summary with no credential. The HTTP path is safe (it uses authenticate_with_key), but the stdio default is fail-open rather than fail-closed.

summary: Retry-After header insert can silently drop the header and a malformed retry value never surfaces, but more importantly the value can panic-fallback to a hardcoded '1'.
failure_scenario: resp.headers_mut().insert("Retry-After", e.retry_after_secs.to_string().parse().unwrap_or_else(|_| "1".parse().unwrap())) parses the numeric seconds into a HeaderValue; numeric strings always parse so the unwrap_or_else branch is dead, but if e.retry_after_secs were ever rendered non-ASCII the inner "1".parse().unwrap() is a reachable-by-construction expect. Low blast radius, but the real correctness gap is that stream_call_handler (line 394) performs the same rate-limit check yet returns the JSON-RPC error WITHOUT the Retry-After header, so the two rate-limited code paths are inconsistent and SSE clients get no backoff hint.

summary: validate_client_capabilities treats any malformed capabilities header as permissive (pass-through), so a client can bypass negotiation by sending garbage.
failure_scenario: parse_capabilities returns Err for a header like 'a2a/1,badformat'; validate_client_capabilities maps that Err to Ok(()) (line 186, 'malformed header -> permissive'). A client that is supposed to be rejected for requiring the disabled a2a capability can instead append one malformed token to the SAME header value and the entire negotiation is skipped, so the request proceeds as if no capabilities were required. Combined with the documented 'permissive when absent' design this means capability negotiation provides no actual enforcement guarantee against a hostile or buggy client — it only helps cooperative ones.

summary: generate_agent_toml interpolates the raw, untrusted company name into a quoted TOML string without escaping, allowing TOML corruption/injection.
failure_scenario: company_name comes from interactive Input with no validation. generate_agent_toml does display_name = "{company_name} Assistant" via format!. A company name containing a double-quote (e.g. Acme "Best" Co) or a newline produces malformed TOML so the agent fails to load on next run, and a crafted value like `X\nhard_stop = true\nfoo = \"` could inject/override config keys (e.g. budget hard_stop, permissions). patch_agent_toml correctly uses toml::Value (safe); only the from-scratch generator is vulnerable. Fix: build the document with toml::Value / toml::to_string like patch_agent_toml does, or escape the string.

summary: macOS service logs() points users at /tmp/duduclaw.*.log but install() configures launchd to write logs to ~/Library/Logs/duduclaw.*.log.
failure_scenario: On macOS, launchd::install writes StandardOutPath/StandardErrorPath to {home}/Library/Logs/duduclaw.stdout.log (line 136-138), but service_logs prints `tail -f /tmp/duduclaw.stdout.log /tmp/duduclaw.stderr.log`. A user running `duduclaw service logs` tails non-existent /tmp files and sees nothing, concluding the service produces no output / is broken. Fix: emit the actual ~/Library/Logs path used by the plist.

summary: macOS service stop SIGKILLs every process found by `lsof -ti :PORT`, which can kill an unrelated process that happens to bind the same port.
failure_scenario: stop() resolves the port from DUDUCLAW_PORT (or default 18789) and calls find_pids_on_port, then terminate_process/kill_process on every returned PID with no verification that the PID is actually a duduclaw process. If the gateway is not running and another local service (or a user's dev server) is bound to that port, `duduclaw service stop` SIGTERMs then SIGKILLs that unrelated process. Fix: verify the process executable/argv is the duduclaw binary before signaling, or stop only the launchctl-managed job.

summary: semantic_conflict_count compares RFC3339 timestamps (stored via to_rfc3339, e.g. '2026-06-18T12:00:00+00:00') against datetime('now','-7 days') which yields SQLite format ('2026-06-11 12:00:00'), so the string-based >= comparison is unreliable.
failure_scenario: Episodic rows are stored with timestamp = entry.timestamp.to_rfc3339() (T separator + '+00:00' offset). The query bound 'datetime(now,-7 days)' produces a space-separated, offset-less string. Lexicographic comparison of e.g. '2026-06-11T08:00:00+00:00' vs '2026-06-11 12:00:00' compares 'T'(0x54) > ' '(0x20), so a row 7 days ago earlier-in-the-day than 'now' is wrongly counted as within the window (and the offset suffix further skews the boundary). The reflection-trigger heuristic over/under-counts near the 7-day edge. search() at line 461-463 already documents and fixes exactly this by binding an RFC3339 now param; the same fix should be applied here.

summary: Fast-keyword matching uses naive substring `combined.contains(kw)`, so short keywords like "hi" match inside unrelated words and wrongly boost confidence toward local tiers.
failure_scenario: The default fast_keywords (config.rs:330) include "hi" and "list". route() lowercases the combined prompt and does `combined.contains("hi")`. A genuinely complex request such as "Please fix this thing and verify the architecture history" contains the substring "hi" (in "this"/"architecture"/"history") and "list" matches "listen"/"realistic", each adding +0.2 confidence. This pushes a query that should escalate to CloudApi/LocalStrong into LocalFast, so a weak local model answers a complex question. The cloud-keyword path has the same substring flaw. Fix: tokenize and match whole words (or word-boundary check) instead of `contains`.

summary: Downloaded GGUF files are never integrity-checked (no SHA/size verification), and the mirror fallback fetches over a third-party host, so a compromised mirror or MITM can deliver a malicious GGUF that is then parsed/loaded by llama.cpp/mistral.rs.
failure_scenario: download_model falls back to mirror_url (hf-mirror.com, a third party) on any primary failure with send_auth=false. Neither path verifies the downloaded bytes against the registry's known size_bytes nor any content hash before renaming .partial → .gguf and feeding it to GGUFLoaderBuilder (mistral_rs.rs:251) / llama.cpp. A malicious or MITM'd mirror can serve a crafted GGUF; malformed GGUF parsing in the native loaders is a memory-safety attack surface. Fix: pin and verify a SHA-256 (or at minimum exact size_bytes) for curated entries and reject mismatches before loading.

summary: Action-claim verification uses an unanchored substring match on params_summary, so a short or prefix target_id is falsely 'verified' against an unrelated tool call.
failure_scenario: verify_claims computes target_match as params_summary.contains(&claim.target_id) (208-211). AGENT_ID_RE permits IDs as short as one char. An agent that outputs e.g. '建立 agent tl' (target_id='tl') is marked Verified by ANY create_agent tool call whose params_summary contains the substring 'tl' (e.g. name=tl-xianwen, or display_name=Title), even though no agent 'tl' was created. This lets tool-use hallucinations slip past the verifier whenever the hallucinated target is a substring of a real call's params, undermining the anti-hallucination guarantee.

summary: resolve_agent_keys hands every agent the global Anthropic API key regardless of allowed_channels, contradicting the module's per-agent isolation claim.
failure_scenario: The module doc (lines 1-5) states '[C-3a] Each agent can only access API keys for its allowed_channels.' Channel tokens are correctly gated by the allowed_channels check (line 41), but the Anthropic API key block (62-78) is added unconditionally with no permission check — an agent with allowed_channels=[] still gets keys['anthropic'] = the master Anthropic key. If/when this resolver is wired into the dispatch path, a restricted sub-agent would receive full billing-capable API credentials. (Currently no production caller consumes AgentKeySet, so this is a latent gap + misleading security contract rather than a live leak.)

summary: Detected SOUL.md drift is logged/audited but never acted upon — no quarantine, pause, or rollback — so a compromised soul keeps running.
failure_scenario: check_soul_integrity_with_audit (505-557) explicitly documents 'the result is reported but not acted upon': on drift it only emits a warn! log and appends a tool_call audit row with tool '_soul_integrity_drift'. The agent continues serving channel traffic with the modified SOUL.md. Combined with the self-heal-on-missing-hash path (soul_guard.rs:168) and SHA-256 being the sole detector, an attacker who modifies a soul faces at most a log line, not enforcement. Also note it uses append_tool_call rather than audit::log_soul_drift, so the dedicated soul_drift Critical event constructor is never fired from the live path.

summary: Agent resolution iterates AgentRegistry's HashMap.values() in non-deterministic order for both trigger and coarse-permission matching, so routing is non-deterministic when multiple agents match.
failure_scenario: registry.list() returns self.agents.values().collect() over a HashMap (registry.rs:179-181). resolve() (resolver.rs:34-52) returns the FIRST agent whose trigger substring-matches (step 1) or whose allowed_channels contains the channel (step 3, line 49). With 2+ agents allowing "discord" (common: most sub-agents allow the same channel), the chosen agent changes between process restarts / rehashes. A message can be handled by agent A today and agent B tomorrow with no config change, producing inconsistent SOUL/memory/permissions for the same conversation.

summary: Two agent directories with the same [agent].name silently overwrite each other in the registry (last scan wins).
failure_scenario: scan() keys the registry on agent.config.agent.name (loaded.insert(name, agent), line 123) rather than the directory name, with no collision check. If two agent dirs declare the same name (copy-paste of agent.toml, or a directory rename without updating name), one agent is silently dropped — it never receives heartbeats, channel routing, or task pulls. Because directory iteration order is non-deterministic, which of the two survives can change across restarts. No WARN is emitted, so the missing agent is invisible to operators.

summary: Trigger matching uses unbounded substring containment, allowing a message to be routed to an unintended (possibly more privileged) agent by embedding its trigger word anywhere in the text.
failure_scenario: match_trigger does text.to_lowercase().contains(trigger.to_lowercase()) (line 60) — no word-boundary or prefix anchoring despite the doc comment claiming '@DuDu at the start'. In a shared channel, a user can craft a message like 'please ignore that, dudu-admin handle this' to force routing to the agent whose trigger is 'dudu-admin' (e.g. a higher-privilege ops agent), bypassing the channel-binding/permission scoping that would otherwise direct them to a restricted agent. Combined with the non-deterministic ordering above, an attacker can also trip ambiguous-trigger collisions.

summary: A scope present in both requires_approval and denied_scopes is routed to approval instead of hard-denied, allowing a denied capability to be escalated via the approval workflow.
failure_scenario: evaluate_permission checks requires_approval_for(scope) FIRST (line 350) and returns require_approval before reaching the denied_scopes check (line 358). PermissionPolicy::validate (policy.rs:143) only rejects allowed/denied overlap, not requires_approval/denied overlap. A policy with denied_scopes:[admin] and requires_approval:[admin] (e.g. via a misconfigured or maliciously-crafted agent YAML that overrides the global denied list) lets an agent obtain 'admin' once an approver approves, defeating the explicit deny.

summary: Policy merge dedups by policy_id only, so an agent-specific policy silently suppresses a global policy of a DIFFERENT type that happens to share the same policy_id.
failure_scenario: In get_policies_for_agent, agent_ids is built from agent_specific policy_ids (line 186-187) and a global policy is dropped if its policy_id collides (line 196). If global.yaml has a Quota policy 'default' and an agent file has a Rate policy 'default', the agent's rate policy entirely removes the global quota enforcement for that agent — the agent loses all token-budget/concurrency limits, with no warning. upsert_policy_with_scope guards against this for runtime upserts (registry.rs:301) but the YAML load/merge path does not.

summary: cleanup_expired never removes Approved/Rejected records, so the pending map grows unboundedly over the process lifetime.
failure_scenario: cleanup_expired retains any record whose status != Expired (line 338). Approved and Rejected decisions are kept forever (the comment says 'keep decided records for history'), but there is no upper bound or eviction. In a long-running gateway that processes thousands of approval decisions, the in-memory HashMap<String, PendingApproval> grows without limit, a slow memory leak with no compaction path.

summary: RuleEngine::from_specs does not dedup rules by id, contradicting the manager comment that claims later same-id specs overwrite earlier ones.
failure_scenario: manager.rs:96-98 documents that 'same-id earlier specs are shadowed by the engine's later compile-and-overwrite by id', and only de-dups inline rules against profile rules via specs.retain. But from_specs (engine.rs:42-61) simply pushes every spec into a flat Vec with no id de-dup. If two profiles in `profiles = ["general", "financial"]` both define an `email` rule (or any shared id), BOTH compile and BOTH fire. Overlap resolution then keeps one by priority/id ordering, so behavior is non-obvious and rule_count/audit rule_id can reflect the wrong rule; it also doubles match work on every redact for every duplicated id. The override-by-id contract operators rely on for profile layering is silently not honored across profiles.

summary: SourceMode::Selective on a non-system-prompt source falls into the full-redact arm, so a 'selective' policy on tool_results/user_input redacts with ALL rules.
failure_scenario: mode_for() maps each source to its configured SourceMode, then redact() matches `SourceMode::On | SourceMode::Selective => {}` (line 107) and proceeds to engine.apply(). engine.apply only restricts to apply_to_system_prompt rules when the Source is SystemPrompt (engine.rs:75). So if an operator sets sources.tool_results = "selective" (a legal SourceMode value) expecting only opted-in rules, every rule fires instead. Selective is only meaningful for system_prompt; on any other source it silently behaves identically to On, which is surprising and can over-redact tool output the operator intended to leave mostly intact.

summary: collect_response (non-interactive) discards trailing bytes after a matched frame instead of re-buffering them, losing the head of the next response on a reused session.
failure_scenario: When parse_frame matches and `accumulator` still holds bytes belonging to a subsequent request, the code at session.rs:642-655 merely logs 'discarding tail bytes (Phase 2 will re-buffer)' and drops them — there is no push-back into PtyHandle's rx_buffer. On a long-lived pooled session where a fast CLI emits the next turn's opening sentinel in the same read window, those leading bytes are permanently lost, so the following invoke can never pair its frame and times out with InvokeTimeout, evicting an otherwise-healthy session.

summary: The blocking reader pump has no consumer between invokes, so a chatty TUI fills the 64-slot channel and the reader thread blocks indefinitely on blocking_send, stalling PTY drainage for idle pooled sessions.
failure_scenario: An interactive `claude` session left idle in the pool keeps emitting spinner/redraw/keepalive bytes. Nothing calls read_until while idle, so reader_rx is never polled; after 64 queued ReadEvent::Chunk entries the reader thread (pty.rs:366) blocks on blocking_send. The PTY master read buffer then backs up toward the kernel pipe limit, and because drain_buffer cannot relieve it (see the rx_buffer finding), the child can eventually block on its own stdout write. The next acquire's first read may also be served stale queued chunks rather than fresh output, increasing latency and mispairing risk. A background drain or larger/unbounded channel with periodic compaction is needed.

summary: In collect_response_interactive a ReadTimeout with an empty drain continues the loop without progress accounting, busy-spinning every min(remaining,3s) and only failing at the outer deadline even when the child has clearly gone silent.
failure_scenario: If the model never emits the closing INTERACTIVE_SENTINEL (e.g. it wrapped the answer in a code fence despite the instruction, or the TUI ate a char of the close sentinel — the exact failure the fixed-sentinel design was meant to avoid still happens for the close marker), read_until returns ReadTimeout, drained is empty, and the loop `continue`s. The session keeps re-reading in 3s slices until the full per-invoke deadline (default 180s) elapses, holding the per-agent semaphore permit the whole time and blocking every other request for that agent for up to 3 minutes before returning InvokeTimeout.

summary: check_bash_command matches only the forward-slash literal `.claude/agents/`, so Windows backslash paths bypass the parallel-hierarchy guard entirely.
failure_scenario: The sentinel is `const SENTINEL: &str = ".claude/agents/"` and the only detection is command.find(SENTINEL). On Windows (a supported platform per the PTY-pool design) an agent runs `mkdir C:\proj\.claude\agents\pm & copy template.toml C:\proj\.claude\agents\pm\agent.toml` — the backslash form contains no `.claude/agents/` substring, so check_bash_command returns NotAgentFile and the PreToolUse hook (cli/lib.rs:1369) allows it. The agent scaffolds a parallel agent hierarchy outside ~/.duduclaw/agents that the guard was specifically built to prevent. The Write/Edit guard also won't catch a `copy`/`cat` shell write because that path goes through Bash, not Write.

summary: The home-relative allow check uses case-sensitive PathBuf::starts_with, so on case-insensitive filesystems a legitimate write to <home>/Agents/<name>/SOUL.md is mis-classified as BlockedOutsideHome.
failure_scenario: check_agent_file_write computes agents_root = home.join("agents") and tests normalized.starts_with(&agents_root), which compares OsStr bytes case-sensitively. On macOS (default case-insensitive APFS) and Windows, `/Users/alice/.duduclaw/Agents/mybot/agent.toml` resolves to the very same canonical directory, but starts_with(".../agents") is false, so the guard returns BlockedOutsideHome and the hook exits 2, blocking a write that is in fact inside the canonical agents dir. This is fail-safe (no security hole) but produces a confusing hard block for a valid path, and the block_message tells the user to use create_agent even though they were writing in the right place.

summary: delay_for_attempt panics with 'cannot sample empty range' when capped delay is 0, reachable on any unvalidated RetryPolicy.
failure_scenario: delay_for_attempt is pub and RetryPolicy is Deserialize/directly-constructible without calling validate(). A policy with initial_delay_ms=0 (e.g. deserialized from a hand-edited TOML, or built via struct literal in tests/integration) yields base=0 → capped=0; rand::thread_rng().gen_range(0.0..0.0) panics on an empty range, crashing the retry path on the first backoff computation instead of degrading gracefully.

summary: max_checkpoints config field is declared 'to prevent OOM' but save() never enforces it, allowing unbounded memory growth.
failure_scenario: CheckpointConfig.max_checkpoints (default 10_000, documented '防止記憶體溢出') is never consulted in save(); records only shrink in maybe_cleanup() which (a) runs at most hourly and (b) only drops expired entries. With the default 24h TTL and many distinct (task_id, agent_id, phase) keys (long-running agents checkpointing every phase), the HashMap grows without bound far past max_checkpoints between/even after cleanups, defeating the stated memory safeguard.

summary: Revocation is not durably recorded; if delete_default() fails, a restart re-activates the revoked license until the next 24h CRL poll.
failure_scenario: `downgrade_to_opensource` (lines 469-479) clears in-memory state and best-effort `storage::delete_default()`, swallowing the error (read-only fs, permission, root-owned file). On gateway restart, `load_and_validate` reloads the still-present, still-signed, not-yet-expired license.json and re-activates the commercial tier. CRL state is never cached locally, so the revoked subscription is honored for up to CRL_POLL_INTERVAL (24h) after every restart, and indefinitely if the control-plane is unreachable. Revocation should be persisted as a local tombstone keyed by subscription_id.

summary: validate() never enforces tier/deployment-mode binding, so a cloud-only tier on a self-host binary (or vice versa) passes; the defined TierModeMismatch error is never produced.
failure_scenario: `LicenseError::TierModeMismatch` (error.rs:57) and `LicenseTier::is_cloud_only`/`is_self_host_only` (tier.rs:67-74) exist but no code path checks them. `License::validate` (lines 115-147) checks version/issued_at/expiry/fingerprint/grace only. A license whose `tier = Business` (cloud-only, designed never to be self-host-issued) installed on a self-hosted binary validates and unlocks the full cloud inheritance chain (`inheritance_chain` Business -> studio -> solo -> hobby), including features the self-host product was never meant to expose. Requires a genuine signature, so it is an issuer-trust gap rather than a forgery, but the documented invariant ('Cloud-only tiers cannot be issued as self-host licenses') is unenforced on the client.

summary: company_ids is parsed and documented as a multi-company query scope but is never applied to any RPC call — search_read/execute_kw always send context {lang:zh_TW} with no allowed_company_ids, so the configured company isolation is a no-op.
failure_scenario: Operator sets agent.toml [odoo] company_ids = [1] expecting the agent to only see/modify company 1's records (per the field doc 'queries are narrowed to these res.company ids'). Because nothing in odoo_pool.rs or mcp.rs injects company_ids/allowed_company_ids into the context or domain, the agent's Odoo user still reads and writes records across every company it has ACL access to. A false sense of cross-company data isolation; the only real boundary is the Odoo user's own ACL.

summary: scrub_odoo_error only truncates to 240 chars; it does not strip URLs/query strings or credentials as the design claims, so a short Odoo error echoing the connection URL or a query-string secret is forwarded verbatim to the dashboard.
failure_scenario: Odoo (or a fronting proxy) returns an error under 240 chars such as 'auth failed for https://odoo.example.com/web?token=abcd1234&db=prod'. scrub_odoo_error returns it unchanged (no redaction, only length cap), and handle_odoo_test surfaces it to the dashboard client. Any credential/token/host embedded in a sub-240-char error message leaks to the UI, contradicting the documented 'avoid leaking ... URLs with query strings' guarantee.

summary: poll_model records the new last_poll timestamp as Utc::now() captured AFTER search_read returns, so any record written during the HTTP round-trip (whose write_date falls between query execution and now()) is permanently skipped on the next poll.
failure_scenario: poll cycle: compute since=T0, issue search_read (domain write_date > T0). The query executes on Odoo at T1; the response returns at T2. A record updated at time t where T1 < t < T2 is not in this batch (already evaluated) and is excluded next time because last_poll is set to T2 > t. The event is silently lost. The fix is to capture now() before issuing the query (and ideally subtract a safety margin), not after. Also Utc::now() vs Odoo server write_date timezone skew compounds this.

summary: extract_frontmatter terminates the YAML block at the first `\n---` substring, so any frontmatter value or comment line beginning with --- truncates the block early and the whole record is silently dropped.
failure_scenario: A person file has a frontmatter value like `display_name: "--- DRAFT ---"` on its own logical line, or a YAML value whose line starts with `---`. body.find("\n---") matches that inner line instead of the real closing fence, so serde_yaml::from_str gets a truncated/malformed block, parse_person_file returns Err, and iter_people warns-and-skips. The person becomes permanently unresolvable (resolve_by_channel returns Ok(None) → treated as a stranger), with no surfaced error to the operator beyond a warn log. Also `\n----------` (a longer markdown HR) is accepted as the closing fence.

summary: Notion HTTP non-2xx response bodies are embedded (up to 200 chars) into IdentityError::Malformed and surfaced verbatim to the LLM/agent via the identity_resolve MCP tool_error path.
failure_scenario: When the Notion provider is wired in, a 4xx/5xx from api.notion.com returns its raw body in `HTTP {status}: {body}`. handle_identity_resolve (mcp.rs ~8495) renders this with tool_error directly into the agent's context, and build_sender_block logs provider errors. Notion error bodies can echo request/property names and HTML, leaking upstream schema/PII into LLM context and logs. Currently latent because only WikiCacheIdentityProvider is constructed in production, but the leak is built into the reusable provider.

summary: load_or_generate sets owner-only permissions only when generating a new jwt_secret; an existing secret file is read and trusted without verifying or repairing its permissions.
failure_scenario: If jwt_secret already exists (line 58 branch) — e.g. restored from a backup, copied with a permissive umask, or written by an older build before the R2 fix — the code reads it (line 59) and immediately uses it as the HS256 signing key with no call to set_owner_only or has_loose_permissions. A world/group-readable jwt_secret lets any local user on the host read the signing key and forge access tokens for any user/role (including admin) by crafting Claims with token_type="access". The only length guard (>=32 bytes) does nothing for confidentiality.

summary: Failed login attempts are only emitted as a warn! tracing line and are never recorded in the auth_audit_log table, leaving brute-force/credential-stuffing attempts invisible to the queryable audit trail.
failure_scenario: In handle_login, a successful login calls db.log_action(..., "login", ...) (server.rs:1173), but the Err branch (line 1130-1136) only does warn!(email, "Login failed") and returns 401. query_audit_log (db.rs:473) therefore can never surface failed-auth events. An operator investigating a suspected account compromise or running anomaly detection over auth_audit_log sees zero failed-login records, so credential-stuffing against admin@local (especially with the default "admin" password) produces no auditable evidence despite the crate advertising an auth audit log.

summary: send_to_bus does not validate chat_id/sender/text, letting a compromised channel plugin inject arbitrary fields (e.g. embedded newlines) into bus_queue.jsonl lines.
failure_scenario: send_to_bus serializes channel/chat_id/sender/text via serde_json (which escapes control chars, so direct line-injection is prevented), but unlike send_message there is no length/charset validation on chat_id or sender. An attacker controlling the upstream channel content can stuff up to ~1 MiB of attacker text into `text`/`sender`, and downstream consumers that interpolate `sender`/`text` into prompts or logs without their own bounds inherit oversized/adversarial input; the per-field absence of validation here is the boundary that should fail fast.

summary: Trigger-word routing uses substring contains() on agent_id, so an agent id that is a substring of any message word mis-routes the message.
failure_scenario: MessageRouter::route (router.rs:59-65) does `message.text.contains(agent_id)`. An agent registered as id `bob` is triggered by any message containing 'bobsled', 'bobbin', or a URL containing 'bob', delivering the message to the wrong agent. (Note: the duduclaw-bus crate is currently not referenced by any other crate per grep, so this is latent/unreachable today, but the logic is wrong if the crate is wired up.)

summary: memory_search HTML-escapes the query before semantic search, degrading recall for any query with quotes/ampersands/angle brackets.
failure_scenario: Client searches query="don't ship & break". _sanitize() turns it into "don't ship & break" before it reaches _search_fn (search.py:111). The embedding/keyword backend now matches the literal entity-encoded tokens instead of the user's words, so legitimately-stored memories are missed — a silent precision/recall loss with no error surfaced.

summary: Daily write quota is enforced check-then-await-then-increment, so concurrent stores from one client can overrun the limit.
failure_scenario: Quota for a client is at used=999/limit=1000. The client fires 5 concurrent memory_store calls. Each calls check_or_raise (line 115) which sees used=999 < 1000 and passes, because increment() (line 136) only runs after each `await self._store_fn` completes. All 5 writes proceed -> used ends at 1004, exceeding the documented hard limit. The quota.py docstring claims the BEFORE-write check guarantees the limit, but the await gap breaks it under asyncio concurrency; a malicious client can exploit this to write past quota.

summary: _set_status / agent_status build agent.toml paths from an unvalidated `name`, unlike agent_create which calls _is_valid_agent_id, allowing path traversal read/write.
failure_scenario: agent_pause('../../../../tmp/evil') -> _set_status() builds toml_path = agents_dir / '../../../../tmp/evil' / 'agent.toml' and, if that file exists, does toml_path.write_text(...) (line 255) outside the agents dir; agent_status() similarly read_text()s arbitrary paths. agent_create() guards against exactly this (line 75) but the status methods omit the check. Currently latent because this Python AgentTools class has no importer in-repo (the live MCP tool is the Rust handle_agent_status), but it is a public method in a shipped package and will become an arbitrary file write/read primitive the moment it is wired in.

summary: A second browser.approval_request arriving while a modal is still open silently overwrites the first request, which is then never answered by the UI.
failure_scenario: The subscribe callback unconditionally does respondedRef.current=false; setRequest(req); setCountdown(...). If agent A's approval is on screen and agent B emits a second approval_request before the user clicks, the first request object is replaced and its countdown state is reset, so the UI never sends browser.respond_approval for request A. The operator believes they are acting on a single prompt; request A is left to the backend timeout auto-deny, and in a busy multi-agent setup approvals can be dropped/confused (operator may approve B thinking it is A). A queue (array of pending requests) is needed instead of a single slot.

summary: Refresh token is persisted in localStorage, making it readable by any successful XSS and not cleared on tab close.
failure_scenario: login() stores data.refresh_token via localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY_REFRESH, ...). localStorage is JS-readable and shared across the origin indefinitely. The frontend currently has no HTML-injection sink, but any future XSS (e.g. a markdown renderer added to KnowledgeHub/WebChat) would let an attacker read the long-lived refresh token and mint access tokens at will, since /api/refresh accepts it without any device/fingerprint binding. An httpOnly+SameSite cookie for the refresh token would remove it from the XSS blast radius.

summary: Vite dev proxy forwards only /ws and /health, but auth-store calls /api/login, /api/refresh, /api/me — so login is broken under `npm run dev`.
failure_scenario: auth-store.apiPost/apiGet fetch relative paths /api/login, /api/refresh, /api/me. The dev server proxy block only contains '/ws' and '/health' targets, so in development these POST/GET requests hit the Vite dev server itself (which returns index.html / 404), making `res.json()` throw or `res.ok` false. A contributor running the dashboard via vite cannot log in at all; the failure surfaces as an opaque 'HTTP 404'/parse error rather than a clear hint. Production (served from the Rust binary, same origin) is unaffected, so this is a dev-only breakage.

summary: handle_tasks_assign builds an `update` JSON value that is never used, then re-builds an equivalent object inline.
failure_scenario: At handlers.rs:7134 `let update = json!({ "assigned_to": agent_id });` is computed and immediately dropped; the next line calls handle_tasks_update with a freshly constructed json!({task_id, assigned_to}). The dead binding is harmless at runtime but signals a copy-paste leftover and produces a dead_code/unused warning; remove the stray `update` binding.

summary: Concurrent confirmations for the same user_id overwrite the prior oneshot sender, silently resolving the earlier request as declined.
failure_scenario: Two computer-use confirmations are requested for the same chat/user before either is answered. wait_for_confirmation inserts into CONFIRMATION_REGISTRY keyed only by user_id; the second insert replaces the first Sender, which is dropped. The first wait_for_confirmation sees Ok(Err(_)) (sender dropped) and returns false, silently declining an operation the user never saw resolved.

summary: resolve_confirmation treats any non-affirmative pending-user message as a decline and consumes the pending confirmation.
failure_scenario: While a confirmation prompt is pending, the user sends an unrelated message (e.g. 'what do you mean?'). resolve_confirmation pops the pending entry and, because is_confirmation_reply returns false for unknown text, sends false through the oneshot — the high-risk action is cancelled and the pending state is gone, even though the user neither confirmed nor explicitly declined. Safe-by-default but confusing and forces the user to restart the flow.

summary: Channel-facing error/feedback strings are English while the product targets zh-TW users; several user-visible Telegram/Slack/Discord status and help messages are not localized.
failure_scenario: On voice/audio transcription failure the user sees "Voice transcription failed — please try again" (telegram.rs:448/458), /help and /status output is English (telegram.rs:715-721, 687), and Discord /agent / /session replies are English while the permission errors are zh-TW — inconsistent. A Taiwanese end user interacting via Telegram receives mixed-language feedback, contradicting the CLAUDE.md zh-TW UX requirement and the warm/approachable brand voice.

summary: Slack message splitting uses byte-length thresholds and byte-based split_message on a string that has had a multi-byte `<@user>` prefix prepended, risking a char-boundary panic / mojibake on long CJK replies.
failure_scenario: After format!("<@{user}> {}", ...) the reply is split when reply.len() > 3900 (bytes) via split_message(&reply, 3900), which does &text[start..chunk_end] using byte arithmetic and only falls back on '\n'. For a long reply with no newline in a 3900-byte window composed of 3-byte CJK chars, chunk_end = start + max_len can land mid-character and &text[start..chunk_end] panics; even when it doesn't panic, the byte cap under-splits relative to Slack's 4000-char (not byte) limit. Reachable with any long Chinese answer lacking newlines.

summary: System-injected hallucination/permission warnings appended to agent responses are English-only and leak internal implementation details to end users on zh-TW channels.
failure_scenario: A sub-agent reply that trips the permission_blocked heuristic (contains '權限' + 'mcp__duduclaw') gets the English block '[DUDUCLAW_SYSTEM:MCP_PERMISSION_BLOCKED] ... The gateway needs to pass --allowedTools ... Please update the gateway binary to fix this.' appended (lines 653-659) and forwarded verbatim to the originating Telegram/LINE/Discord user. A Taiwanese end user sees raw CLI-flag jargon and an internal 'update the gateway binary' instruction in English, violating the zh-TW channel UX expectation and exposing internal config details.

summary: cap_rust_log_verbosity does substring replacement of 'trace' and corrupts target names containing that substring.
failure_scenario: cap_rust_log_verbosity(filter) does filter.replace("trace","debug"). A legitimate RUST_LOG like `my_tracer=info` or a crate/module named with 'trace' (e.g. `opentelemetry_trace=warn`, `tracexport=info`) becomes `my_debugr=info` / `opentelemetry_debug=warn`, breaking the EnvFilter directive for the worker so its logging target silently does not match and those logs vanish. The intended cap (downgrade the trace LEVEL) should parse level tokens, not blind substring-replace.

summary: graduate_skill_to_disk builds the destination filename directly from skill.name with no path-component validation, unlike the MCP path which uses is_safe_path_component.
failure_scenario: graduate_skill_to_disk does `agent_skills_dir.join(format!("{}.md", skill.name))` (line 317) with no kebab/`..`/`/` check. Today the only constructor (from_synthesized) carries a name that passed parse_synthesis_response kebab validation, so it is safe in current call sites; but the function is `pub` and the SandboxedSkill.name field is public and Deserialize, so a future caller that deserializes a trial from disk or constructs SandboxedSkill directly with name = `../../.claude/settings` would write outside the SKILLS dir. The sibling MCP graduation path correctly guards with is_safe_path_component (mcp.rs:5533); this helper should reuse the same validation rather than trusting the name.

summary: trajectory_id uses a byte-index slice session_id[..8.min(len)] that can panic if a session_id has a non-ASCII char straddling byte boundary 8.
failure_scenario: The expression &session_id[..8.min(session_id.len())] slices on byte offsets, not char boundaries. Today's channel session_ids are ASCII-prefixed (discord:/line:/telegram:) so byte 8 always lands inside ASCII and it is safe in practice. But this is a latent panic: any future caller whose session_id is derived from a group/room id or username containing a multi-byte UTF-8 char near offset 8 would hit 'byte index 8 is not a char boundary' and panic the spawned trajectory-collection task. Use session_id.chars().take(8) to make it boundary-safe.

summary: On a write error the logger nulls the file state but does NOT re-attempt the current event, so the event that triggered the error is dropped even though the handle is recoverable.
failure_scenario: In log() at logger.rs:223-231, if `state.file.write_all` returns Err (e.g. transient ENOSPC that later clears, or a stale handle after external log rotation/deletion), the code logs the error and sets `*guard = None` so the *next* call reopens — but the current `event` is silently lost rather than retried after reopen. For an audit/security log this is a gap: the very events most likely to coincide with disk pressure (incident bursts) are the ones dropped, with only a stderr line as evidence.

summary: Autopilot `notify` action interpolates rule-supplied chat_id directly into the Discord API path with no numeric-snowflake validation (unlike the reminder path).
failure_scenario: In send_channel_text the Discord branch builds `https://discord.com/api/v10/channels/{chat_id}/messages` (line 757-758) from action.chat_id with no validation. The reminder scheduler validates Discord chat_id is a numeric snowflake (reminder_scheduler.rs:261, is_valid_discord_chat_id), but the autopilot notify path skips that check. A malformed/crafted chat_id (e.g. containing `/` or `..`) alters the request path. Rules are operator-created via dashboard RPC so exposure is limited, but the inconsistency means a typo'd or hostile rule sends to an unintended Discord endpoint instead of failing fast with a clear error; telegram/line chat_id are body fields and lower risk.

summary: all_agents_summary and recent_records cast SUM(output_tokens) i64 directly to u64 without safe_u64, inconsistent with sibling columns.
failure_scenario: Lines 526 and 567 use `row.get::<_, i64>(5)? as u64` for output_tokens while every other aggregate column funnels through safe_u64() (which clamps negatives to 0). If a stored row ever held a negative value (corrupt write, manual DB edit, or a future bug), the direct cast on these two columns wraps to a huge u64 instead of clamping to 0 like the others, skewing the displayed totals. Low impact because tokens are inserted as u64 today, but it's an unguarded boundary the rest of the module deliberately guards.

summary: handle_wiki_write lacks the canonicalize() symlink-escape guard that handle_wiki_read applies, so a symlink inside the wiki dir lets a write escape the directory.
failure_scenario: handle_wiki_read verifies full_path.canonicalize() stays under wiki_dir (lines 7392-7395). handle_wiki_write only runs validate_wiki_page_path (blocks '..' and absolute paths) and then writes via temp+rename to wiki_dir.join(page_path) with no canonicalization. If any path component under the wiki dir is a symlink pointing outside (e.g. a pre-existing 'entities' -> /etc symlink), a write to entities/x.md follows the link and writes outside the wiki sandbox; the read path would have caught the same escape.

summary: Key age computed via num_days() as u64 mis-handles future-dated created_at, masking clock-skew/misconfigured keys.
failure_scenario: age = Utc::now().signed_duration_since(entry.created_at); days_old = age.num_days() as u64. If created_at is in the future (clock skew across machines, or an operator typo writing a future date), num_days() returns a negative i64 which casts to a huge u64 (e.g. -1 -> 18446744073709551615), so days_old > 30 triggers and the key is rejected as KeyExpired with an absurd day count in the user-facing message ('API key expired (18446744073709551615 days old, max 30)'). The rotation policy code path at mcp_auth_strategy.rs:206 correctly uses .max(0); authenticate_with_key does not, so the two age calculations disagree and the error message leaks a nonsense value.

summary: store_temporal's comment claims the new row's `supersedes` back-pointer is the 'most-recent superseded id', but the selecting query has no ORDER BY, so existing.into_iter().next() picks an arbitrary row when more than one active row shares the triple.
failure_scenario: If two+ currently-valid rows exist for (agent_id, subject, predicate) (a state the code itself warns about at line 292), all of them get superseded_by = new id, but the new row's `supersedes` is set to whichever id SQLite happened to return first (undefined order). The forward chain (superseded_by) stays intact and get_at/get_history still work via valid_from ordering, but the documented invariant ('most-recent') is false and the back-pointer is non-deterministic. Either ORDER BY valid_from DESC in the SELECT or update the comment.

summary: shared.rs (and federated.rs) are not declared in lib.rs, so they never compile; shared.rs calls crate::search::sanitize_fts5_query which does not exist in search.rs — a latent compile error masked only because the module is dead code.
failure_scenario: SharedMemoryEngine references crate::search::sanitize_fts5_query (search.rs has no such function). Because lib.rs declares neither `mod shared` nor `mod federated`, the 967 LOC of shared/federated memory code is dead and the broken reference is never type-checked. The moment anyone adds `pub mod shared;` to lib.rs to wire up cross-agent shared memory, the crate fails to build. Either delete the dead files or wire them up and add the missing sanitizer.

summary: MlxBridge::is_available caches the first probe result in a OnceCell forever, so a transient failure (mlx_lm not yet importable) permanently disables MLX for the whole process lifetime.
failure_scenario: is_available uses available_cache.get_or_init. If the very first probe runs before the Python env / mlx_lm is ready (e.g. venv still initializing, or a transient Python error), it caches `false`. Every later call — including evolution reflections hours later after the env is fixed — returns the cached false, so MLX is never used and reflections silently fall back to the paid Claude API with no retry and no log indicating the cache is stale. Fix: cache only positive results, or use a TTL'd recheck instead of a permanent OnceCell.

summary: start() has a check-then-set TOCTOU: it reads ServerState under a read lock, drops it, then sets Starting, so two concurrent start() calls can both spawn a llamafile process.
failure_scenario: select_best_mode (manager.rs:222) calls lf.start(None) concurrently with an explicit manager.start_llamafile()/dashboard trigger. Both callers observe state != Running under the read lock (line 122-126), both proceed, both spawn `claude`-style llamafile subprocesses binding the same port. The second child overwrites self.child (line 195); the first child is dropped (kill_on_drop kills it) but only after both raced to bind the port, producing a transient EADDRINUSE and a misleading `llamafile server failed to start within 30s` error. Fix: hold a single write lock / use compare-and-set on the state across the check-and-transition.

summary: vault_token_enc is documented and parsed but never decrypted or used to build a Vault adapter anywhere, so operators following the docs get plaintext-only auth or a non-functional secret backend.
failure_scenario: SecretManagerConfig exposes vault_token_enc 'base64(AES-256-GCM encrypted token)' (165) and the module doc (24-25) tells operators to 'use vault_token_enc in production'. A repo-wide grep shows no code decrypts vault_token_enc nor constructs VaultHttpAdapter from this config (only tests parse it). An operator who sets only vault_token_enc (per the docs) ends up with no usable Vault token, while the safe-looking guidance implies their token is protected. The whole SecretManager backend selection appears unwired into the runtime.

summary: Prompt-injection scanner is purely substring/keyword based and trivially evadable, yet blocked input only swaps the reply text without recording an audit event in the channel path.
failure_scenario: scan_input matches fixed English-only phrases (RULES, 31-117); paraphrases, translations (zh-TW '忽略先前指示'), or interleaving non-ZW filler ('ignore, uh, previous instructions') bypass every rule, so a determined injection passes with risk_score 0. Separately, in channel_reply.rs:842 a blocked message just returns a zh-TW warning string and never calls audit::log_injection_detected, so the convenience constructor's Critical/Warning audit trail (audit.rs:163) is not produced on the live block path — forensic review will not see the block. Low because it is defense-in-depth, not the only control.

summary: LeastCost strategy always selects the first available OAuth account, never rotating among multiple OAuth accounts.
failure_scenario: In select() under LeastCost, OAuth accounts are filtered and Some(*oauth[0]) is returned unconditionally (line 405-407), ignoring priority, last_used, and total_requests. With several OAuth (Pro/Max) accounts configured, the first one is hammered for every request until it hits a rate-limit cooldown, then the next is used — load is never spread, so subscription quota on the first account exhausts far faster than necessary and other accounts sit idle.

summary: consume_tokens computes new_total with saturating_add but compares > budget instead of >=, allowing the very last allocation to land exactly on the budget; combined with check_token_budget using >= the two APIs disagree at the boundary.
failure_scenario: consume_tokens denies only when new_total > daily_token_budget (line 131), so consuming exactly up to the budget succeeds. check_token_budget (line 170) denies when token_used >= budget. After consume_tokens brings token_used to exactly the budget, a subsequent check_token_budget returns TokenBudgetExhausted while another consume_tokens(0-token) would still pass at the boundary — inconsistent enforcement semantics between the consume path and the check path that callers may rely on for gating.

summary: Restoring an expired token emits a RestoreOk audit event even though no cleartext was revealed (only an expired placeholder).
failure_scenario: In restore(), the expired branch (entry.original == None) emits AuditEvent::RestoreOk with target `{target_str}#expired` (lines 248-253). RestoreOk is the same event used for genuine reveals, so an auditor grepping RestoreOk to count actual PII disclosures will overcount: every expired-token restore inflates the reveal metric even though nothing sensitive was returned. A distinct event (e.g. RestoreExpired) would keep the audit trail accurate for incident review.

summary: Taiwan national-ID rule has no word boundary and no checksum, so IDs embedded in longer alphanumeric runs are missed (false-negative leak).
failure_scenario: tw_national_id pattern is `[A-Z][12]\d{8}` with no `\b`/anchoring and no Taiwan ID checksum. A value like `XA123456789` or an ID concatenated inside a token/URL (`refA123456789xyz`) — common in tool JSON results — still matches the substring `A123456789`, which is fine, but the inverse case `0A123456789` where the leading char is a digit means the regex engine still finds `A123456789`; however IDs written with full-width digits, with separators (`A1-2345-6789`), or lowercased (`a123456789`) are NOT matched and pass through un-redacted. Given this is the 'strict' Taiwan profile whose whole purpose is national-ID protection, these formatting variants are realistic leak paths for the highest-sensitivity field.

summary: child_group is built from PtyHandle::pid() which can be None, so on Unix ChildGroup process-group termination is silently skipped and only portable-pty's single-process kill runs.
failure_scenario: If portable_pty's Child::process_id() returns None (observed on some ConPTY/edge spawn paths), pid.map(ChildGroup::new) yields None at session.rs:199. shutdown() then finds child_group == None and never sends SIGTERM to the process group (session.rs:679), so helper subprocesses claude spawned (Bash tools, MCP servers, python eval) are not reaped by the gentle path — they only get SIGHUP via slave-fd close, which some processes ignore, leaking orphaned descendants per evicted session.

summary: RuntimeType::parse silently maps any unknown provider string to Claude, masking typos in agent.toml [runtime] provider with no warning.
failure_scenario: An operator writes `provider = "gemeni"` (typo) or `provider = "claude-code"` in agent.toml. parse() trims+lowercases and falls through the match to the `_ => Self::Claude` arm, returning Claude with no log. The agent silently runs on the wrong runtime backend; the operator believes Gemini/Codex is in use. Because there is no diagnostic, this is only discovered by observing wrong model behaviour or cost attribution.

summary: after_call success path increments consecutive_successes even when no probe was inflight, letting spurious/unpaired success reports prematurely close the breaker.
failure_scenario: In HALF_OPEN, after_call(success=true) does probe_inflight.saturating_sub(1) (clamps at 0) but still increments consecutive_successes unconditionally. If after_call(true) is invoked more times than before_call admitted probes (mispairing, or a retry helper reporting success twice), consecutive_successes reaches probe_success_required and the breaker closes (clearing the window and opened_at) without the configured number of genuinely-admitted probe successes, recovering an unhealthy dependency too early.

summary: expand_home silently returns the literal '~/.duduclaw' when dirs::home_dir() is None, so home_dir-dependent work_dir validation always rejects with a misleading message.
failure_scenario: On a launchd/systemd-spawned worker with no HOME set, dirs::home_dir() returns None; expand_home (main.rs:101-108) returns PathBuf '~/.duduclaw' unchanged. That literal path is passed as config.home_dir=Some('~/.duduclaw'). In handle_invoke (server.rs:506) canonicalize_work_dir joins '~/.duduclaw/agents' which cannot canonicalize, so every Invoke carrying work_dir is rejected with 'work_dir must canonicalize inside <home>/agents/' even for legitimate agent dirs — the operator gets a path-traversal-style error instead of a clear 'HOME not resolvable' diagnostic, and the token file is also written to a literal './~/.duduclaw' relative dir.

summary: work_dir divergence is logged only when the cached session has a Some spawn_cwd; the common case where the first invoke had no work_dir (spawn_cwd None) and a later invoke supplies one is silently ignored.
failure_scenario: Agent 'agnes' first invokes with work_dir=None, so spawn_session_default leaves opts.cwd=None and session.spawn_cwd() returns None. A later invoke for the same agent supplies work_dir=<home>/agents/agnes (so .mcp.json / CLAUDE.md should be discovered). Because work_dir is not part of the cache_key, the cached session is reused at its original (worker) cwd, but the divergence guard at server.rs:554 requires (Some(requested), Some(actual)); with actual=None the branch is skipped, so no warning fires. The agent silently runs without its per-agent MCP/CLAUDE.md config and there is zero operator-visible signal.

summary: Bearer-token authorisation runs after the JSON body is fully deserialized into Request, so unauthenticated callers can drive request parsing work before the auth check.
failure_scenario: rpc_handler's signature `Json(req): Json<Request>` makes axum deserialize the body (up to the 512KB DefaultBodyLimit) before rpc_handler's body — and thus before authorise() — runs. An unauthenticated local process can repeatedly POST 512KB JSON bodies forcing full parse/allocation per request before being rejected with 401. The body limit caps the blast radius, so this is low severity, but the auth gate is logically positioned after untrusted-input parsing rather than before it.

summary: registry_keys log field reports 0 or 1 based on is_empty(), misrepresenting the actual key count during rotation.
failure_scenario: Line 118 logs `registry_keys = registry.is_empty().then_some(0usize).unwrap_or(1)`. A registry holding two rotated issuer keys (v1+v2) logs `registry_keys=1`, and any non-empty registry always logs 1. During a key-rotation incident an operator inspecting startup logs to confirm both keys loaded gets a misleading count; the value should be `registry.len()` (no such accessor exists on PublicKeyRegistry, so one must be added).

summary: LicenseSnapshot::from_state calls generate_fingerprint() (hostname + MAC enumeration) on every dashboard snapshot request.
failure_scenario: `from_state` (line 193) invokes `generate_fingerprint()`, which calls `gethostname` and `mac_address::get_mac_address()` (interface enumeration syscalls) synchronously on every `snapshot()` call. Each dashboard `license.status` RPC / metrics scrape re-walks network interfaces though the fingerprint is process-stable; should be computed once and cached (e.g. OnceLock) rather than recomputed per request.

summary: write()/count() coerce non-bool/non-int RPC results to false/0 with unwrap_or, swallowing protocol-level anomalies; a write that returns an unexpected shape is reported as a benign 'false' rather than an error.
failure_scenario: If Odoo's write returns something other than a JSON bool (e.g. an error-shaped result that still parsed as result rather than error, or a future API change), result.as_bool().unwrap_or(false) yields Ok(false). The caller (odoo_crm_update_stage) treats Ok(_) as success and reports 'Lead moved to stage', so a non-applied write is indistinguishable from success to the agent/user. Prefer surfacing an error when the shape is unexpected.

summary: resolve_by_channel only inspects results[0] from the Notion query, silently ignoring the case where the channel-handle filter matches multiple distinct people.
failure_scenario: If two Notion People rows share the same Discord/Line ID (data-entry error or a deliberately duplicated handle), build_channel_filter's rich_text.equals returns both rows, but `rows[0]` is taken with no warning. Notion does not guarantee a stable ordering for unsorted queries, so which person is resolved is arbitrary — the same silent-ambiguity / impersonation class as the wiki-cache first-match bug, with no diagnostic that the handle was non-unique.

summary: conn() fallback uses self.pool[0].lock().unwrap(), which panics on a poisoned mutex and can cascade into a permanent auth outage.
failure_scenario: If any thread panics while holding a pooled Connection guard (e.g. a rusqlite closure panicking), that Mutex becomes poisoned. With POOL_SIZE=2, if both connections' try_lock fail to return a usable guard and the fallback pool[0] is poisoned, lock().unwrap() (line 43) panics. Because conn() is the single chokepoint for every auth DB operation (login, refresh, ACL checks), a poisoned pool[0] turns every subsequent login/refresh into a panic instead of a recoverable error, effectively a denial of service for all authentication.

summary: ensure_default_admin comment claims "a single transaction to avoid TOCTOU race (HIGH-3 fix)" but no transaction is opened; correctness relies solely on the UNIQUE(email) constraint plus INSERT OR IGNORE.
failure_scenario: The comment at line 525 asserts transactional protection, but the body only holds a MutexGuard on one pooled connection and issues a bare COUNT followed by a separate INSERT OR IGNORE (no BEGIN/COMMIT). With POOL_SIZE=2, two concurrent callers can each acquire a different connection, both read count==0, and both attempt the insert; the actual guard against a duplicate admin is the UNIQUE email + INSERT OR IGNORE, not a transaction. The code is functionally safe today, but a future maintainer trusting the comment could remove the UNIQUE constraint or the post-insert verify, silently reintroducing the race the comment claims is already handled.

summary: key_press parses combos by splitting on '+', so a literal '+' key (e.g. zoom-in shortcut 'ctrl++') is parsed as an empty final segment and silently produces no keystroke.
failure_scenario: key_str.split('+') on 'ctrl++' yields ["ctrl","",""]; the final part is an empty string, parse_enigo_key("") hits the `other` arm and returns None, so the '+' key is never pressed — the agent's requested 'ctrl++' (browser zoom) does nothing, and no error is returned, leaving the computer-use action silently failed.

summary: Skill secret-detection regex ghp_[a-zA-Z0-9]{36} misses GitHub fine-grained PATs (github_pat_*) and the modern 40+ char ghp tokens, so leaked credentials pass vetting.
failure_scenario: An agent-generated skill embeds a fine-grained PAT like github_pat_11ABCDEFG0... or a newer ghp_ token longer/with underscores. The sensitive_data patterns only match legacy ghp_ + exactly 36 base62 chars and sk-..., so vet_skill returns PASS and vet_and_activate (line 178) writes the skill live with the secret intact — the security scan that is meant to catch credential leakage gives a false sense of safety.

summary: Insert success is detected by exact string match 'INSERT 0 1', which silently undercounts when asyncpg returns an oid form like 'INSERT <oid> 1'.
failure_scenario: asyncpg's Connection.execute returns the raw command tag. For tables created WITH OIDS (or future driver/format changes) the tag is 'INSERT <oid> 1' rather than 'INSERT 0 1'. The equality check at line 95 then never matches, so inserted_count stays 0 even though rows were inserted, making take_memory_snapshot report {'inserted': 0} and corrupting the retention-rate baseline that depends on the snapshot count. A token-count comparison (result.split()[-1] == '1') would be robust.

summary: Login surfaces the raw server error string verbatim, which may be English/technical and is not localized to zh-TW.
failure_scenario: On a failed login the catch sets error to err.message, which is whatever data.error the server returned (e.g. 'invalid credentials', 'HTTP 401') and renders it directly. The project's audience is zh-TW and the rest of the page uses intl.formatMessage, so a Chinese-speaking user gets an inconsistent English/technical message with no localized 'account or password incorrect' phrasing and no distinction between wrong-password vs server-down.
